The explicit harmonization of the ASR quantizer with the TTS codec's discrete acoustic space is also a non-trivial mechanism not commonly seen in prior work. Method B's strongest novel element is the layer partitioning curriculum that separates CTC/contrastive objectives for lower encoder layers from seq2seq objectives for upper layers, combined with the inference-time retrieval-augmented bias-aware decoder using vector-indexed domain vocabulary. However, Method B's components are largely well-established combinations (XLS-R/WavLM adaptation, synthetic TTS filtering, pseudo-labeling with confidence filtering, shallow fusion LM) assembled in a systematic but not particularly original way. Method A's round-trip TTS-ASR consistency loop with token attribution scoring as a pseudo-label quality signal, and the codec space harmonization between TTS and ASR quantizers, represent a more original mechanism that would be harder to find in existing literature.

The failure mode is the lack of transcribed audio for low-resource/specialized domains, requiring synthetic data generation and/or transfer learning to achieve competitive ASR accuracy. Both methods engage this failure mode directly through similar mechanisms: multilingual pretrained encoder adaptation, TTS-based synthetic data generation with quality filtering, pseudo-labeling of unlabeled audio, and curriculum fine-tuning on scarce real labels. Method A adds a distinctive token-level attribution alignment mechanism for TTS quality control and round-trip consistency verification for pseudo-labels, creating a more tightly coupled causal pathway from the problem (unreliable synthetic data) to the solution (attribution-based filtering). Method B covers the same core mechanisms but distributes them across more components (layer partitioning, inference-time biasing, LM fusion) that address peripheral aspects rather than the central failure mode. Both methods trace a clear pathway from problem to solution, but Method A's mechanisms more consistently and directly target the core challenge of ensuring synthetic data quality and pseudo-label reliability when real labeled data is scarce.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of cross-domain contrastive learning with structure-from-motion-guided positive pairing, a meta-trained latent dynamics context encoder with online probing routines, and a DAgger-lite risk-critic-triggered correction loop — particularly the SfM-guided cross-domain positive pairing for the visual encoder and the safe probing sequence for dynamics latent initialization. Method B's strongest novel element is the use of digital-twin state replay to reconstruct paired sim-real segments for multi-level semantic consistency alignment, combined with SAM2-tracked object-centric masking and mixed CLIP/DINOv2 backbone fusion. Both methods share a broadly similar two-stage privileged-to-visual distillation structure with few-shot real adaptation. Method A's SfM-guided cross-domain positive pairing and the online probing-based dynamics latent estimation are somewhat more distinctive combinations not commonly seen together, but Method B's use of SAM2-tracked foreground masking combined with digital-twin state replay for paired sim-real alignment is also a fairly specific and less-precedented mechanism. Overall, Method A introduces more layered novel components (the SfM-guided contrastive pairing, the probing-based latent initialization, and the risk-critic DAgger-lite loop), giving it a slight edge in novelty over Method B's more incremental combination of existing techniques.

The failure mode is sim-to-real transfer of visuomotor policies due to physics, sensor, and dynamics mismatch, with minimal real-world demonstrations. Method A directly engages both the visual domain gap (via cross-domain contrastive pretraining with real unlabeled video, MMD alignment, and self-supervised adaptation) and the dynamics/sensor mismatch (via a latent dynamics identifier, meta-trained context encoder, and few-shot residual adaptation), with traceable causal pathways from each failure source to specific method steps. Method B also addresses both gaps through privileged RL with dynamics randomization, visual distillation with domain randomization and cross-domain feature alignment from few real demos, and meta-learned on-hardware adaptation. Both methods are comparably structured and directly engage the named failure modes with similar mechanisms: visual alignment via cross-domain losses, dynamics adaptation via meta-learning and latent/adaptor modules, and minimal real data usage via few-shot anchoring. The causal pathways in both are equally traceable and direct, with neither offering a substantially more targeted engagement of the core failure mode.

Method A's strongest novel element is the Neural Controlled Differential Equation (NCDE) for continuous-time multisensory fusion in visuomotor policies, combined with the VIB-style uncertainty signal driving active demonstration aggregation with pseudo-corrections from a simple goal-directed controller. The NCDE applied to asynchronous multi-rate sensor fusion in sim-to-real transfer is a relatively uncommon combination. Method B's strongest novel element is the cross-domain feature alignment using paired sim-real segments reconstructed by replaying measured states in a digital twin, combined with meta-learning for rapid on-hardware dynamics adaptation. However, Method B's components—privileged-to-visual distillation, MAML-style meta-learning for sim-to-real, domain randomization with pretrained visual backbones (CLIP/DINOv2), and feature alignment losses—are each well-established in the literature and their combination, while competent, follows a fairly predictable recipe. Method A's use of NCDEs for continuous-time control with asynchronous sensing, combined with the uncertainty-guided pseudo-demonstrator DAgger variant and the split latent architecture, represents a more unusual and harder-to-find combination in prior sim-to-real work.

The failure mode is sim-to-real transfer of visuomotor policies with minimal real-world demonstrations. Method A addresses this through a multi-component pipeline: invariant latent representations to separate domain-specific from task-relevant features, visual alignment via lightweight adapters using unlabeled real data, and active demonstration aggregation that minimizes labeled real data needs. Method B addresses it through privileged-to-visual distillation, cross-domain feature alignment using paired sim-real frames, and meta-learned on-hardware adaptation. Both methods engage the failure mode directly. However, Method B's causal pathway is more traceable: it explicitly separates visual gap (Stage 2 distillation + alignment loss) from dynamics mismatch (meta-learned adaptation), and each component maps directly to a named aspect of the sim-to-real failure mode. Method A's mechanisms are also valid but the pathway through CDE-based continuous-time control (Step 2) is a peripheral engagement with the core sim-to-real gap rather than a direct resolution of it, making the overall causal chain less tight. Method B's cross-domain feature alignment using paired sim-real frames directly addresses the visual mismatch with minimal real data, and the meta-learning component directly addresses dynamics mismatch with minimal real rollouts.

Method A's strongest novel element is the coarse-to-fine "last-inch corrective module" trained from a single real demonstration combined with a Variational Information Bottleneck (VIB) applied to the visual encoder for domain-agnostic latent alignment across sim and real. The VIB for sim-to-real domain alignment is a relatively uncommon framing in this context. Method B's strongest novel element is the paired sim-real reconstruction for semantic consistency loss (replaying measured states in the digital twin to create paired frames) combined with a mixed CLIP+DINOv2 backbone with any-resolution tiling. However, Method B's components—meta-learning, domain randomization, CLIP/DINOv2 fusion, SAM2 masking, FiLM adaptors—are largely well-established combinations in the robotics transfer literature. Method A's VIB-based domain-agnostic latent space for sim-to-real transfer and the single-demo last-inch corrective module represent a more distinctive combination not commonly seen together in this exact framing, giving it a slight edge in novelty over Method B's more incremental assembly of known techniques.

The failure mode is the sim-to-real gap in visuomotor policies caused by physics, sensor, and dynamics mismatch, requiring transfer with minimal real demonstrations. Both methods address this through similar mechanisms: privileged RL pretraining, visual distillation with domain randomization, cross-domain feature alignment using few real demos, and meta-learned on-hardware adaptation. Method A uses a cross-domain feature alignment loss pulling sim-real encoder features together at multiple levels, plus meta-learning for dynamics adaptation, directly targeting both visual and dynamics gaps. Method B uses a variational information bottleneck to encourage domain-agnostic latent representations, photorealistic digital twin construction, and an optional contact-critical corrective module. Both trace causal pathways from the problem to specific steps. However, Method A's cross-domain feature alignment loss with paired sim-real frames more directly and mechanistically addresses the visual domain gap with minimal real data, and its meta-learning component more explicitly targets the dynamics mismatch with few rollouts. Method B's VIB is a reasonable approach but is less directly tied to the specific failure mode of dynamics mismatch, and the photorealistic twin construction, while helpful, requires more setup rather than directly resolving transfer with minimal real demos.

Method A's strongest novel element is the multi-faceted slot atomizer with typed VQ codebooks combined with a Prolog-style program inducer that abstracts successful traces into reusable composition templates, plus adversarial foil training for atom boundary hardening. However, each of these components (slot attention, VQ codebooks, neuro-symbolic program induction, curriculum learning, CLIP-style alignment) exists in prior literature and the combination, while elaborate, follows a fairly predictable assembly of known pieces. Method B's strongest novel element is the structural EM loop that alternates between program structure inference and module/codebook updates, combined with algebraic consistency checks (associativity/commutativity enforced via synthetic recombination), interventional stability training on counterfactual perturbations, and a principled split-merge library management procedure under MDL criteria. The algebraic invariance objectives and the interventional stability loss applied specifically to discrete module tokens represent a more unusual combination not easily found in prior neuro-symbolic or program induction literature. Both methods are elaborate assemblies, but Method B's combination of algebraic checks, interventional counterfactual training, and the EM-based library management with split-merge is harder to surface as a prior published combination.

The failure mode is that learned representations are continuous and entangled, preventing discrete, reusable components that support compositional reasoning over novel combinations. Method A directly addresses this by inducing a VQ codebook of discrete module tokens, enforcing reuse via entropy regularization, adding explicit disentanglement objectives (contrastive subproblem consistency, interventional stability, HSIC/MMD independence penalties), and testing generalization by freezing the library and evaluating parser-only adaptation on novel compositions—each step traces back to the core problem of discretizing and disentangling entangled continuous representations. Method B also uses VQ discretization and slot-based parsing, but its mechanism is more oriented toward grounding atoms to language/vision evidence and building a Prolog-style execution layer; the disentanglement of continuous representations is less directly targeted, with more emphasis on multi-modal alignment and curriculum learning. Method A's causal pathway from entangled continuous representations → VQ discretization → typed modules → explicit disentanglement losses → compositional generalization testing is more tightly and completely traced to the named failure mode.

Method A's strongest novel element is the multi-faceted slot atomizer with typed VQ codebooks combined with a Prolog-style latent program inducer that abstracts successful traces into reusable composition templates — this is a fairly elaborate pipeline but draws heavily on established components: slot attention, VQ-VAE, neural module networks/program synthesis, and CLIP-style alignment. The combination is sophisticated but each piece has clear precedents. Method B's strongest novel element is the dual shared/domain-specific prototype bank with a distinction-maximization layer, combined with a mix-and-separate augmentation that trains auxiliary heads to recover constituent factors from composed inputs, and a dynamic library management system (uncertainty-driven atom instantiation and merging via Bradley-Terry ranking). The mix-and-separate augmentation as a training signal for disentangling atoms, combined with the dynamic library growth/consolidation mechanism governed by an uncertainty head, represents a more original combination that is harder to find in prior literature as a unified system. Both methods are elaborate, but Method B's core mechanisms (distinction-maximization + mix-and-separate + dynamic library management) are less directly traceable to existing named methods than Method A's pipeline.

The failure mode is that learned representations are continuous and entangled, preventing discrete, reusable components that support compositional reasoning over novel combinations. Method A addresses this by converting continuous internal states into discrete atoms via a codebook/router mechanism, with explicit losses for disentanglement (group-sparse regularizer, pairwise discriminative objective, mix-and-separate augmentation) and a planner that composes atoms into execution graphs—directly targeting both the discretization and the compositional reuse aspects. Method B addresses it by parsing inputs into typed discrete slots via vector-quantized codebooks, grounding them to language, and composing via a latent program layer with Prolog-style planning and reusable templates. Both methods directly engage the failure mode: discretization via VQ/codebooks and compositional reasoning via explicit planners. However, Method B more directly traces the causal pathway from entangled continuous representations to discrete typed slots (with type-specific codebooks, slot extractors, and facet embeddings) and then to compositional reasoning (typed operators, program traces, template abstraction), with each step explicitly addressing a specific aspect of the failure mode. Method A is also highly direct but spends considerable description on peripheral mechanisms (domain banks, uncertainty heads, curriculum details) that are less tightly coupled to the core failure mode.

Method A's strongest novel element is the multi-faceted vector-quantized slot system (Atomizer) with typed codebooks, per-slot view embeddings aggregated via max pooling, and the subsequent abstraction of successful execution traces into reusable typed composition templates — this combination of object-centric VQ discretization with facet-based retrieval and template induction is not a standard published pipeline. Method B's strongest novel element is the process preference learning with dynamic, state-dependent value margins computed from structured symbolic states rather than natural language, combined with automatic macro-operator abstraction triggered by information-gain stalling. However, Method B is largely an assembly of well-established components: LLM-based program synthesis, DSL-constrained decoding, teacher-student distillation, DPO/preference learning, and backward chaining — each individually well-documented. Method A introduces a more original architectural combination (typed VQ codebooks with multi-view facets, evidence-grounded slot supervision, and template library induction from traces) that is harder to find as a unified prior work, whereas Method B's novelty is more incremental refinement of existing LLM reasoning pipelines.

The failure mode is that learned representations are continuous and entangled, preventing discrete, reusable components that support compositional reasoning over novel combinations. Method A addresses this by constraining outputs to a typed DSL with discrete operator calls, but its primary mechanism is a program-induction pipeline built on top of an LLM—the representations themselves remain continuous; discreteness is enforced only at the interface level through schema constraints and operator signatures. Method B directly attacks the entanglement problem at the representation level: it uses vector-quantized codebooks to convert continuous slot embeddings into discrete integer tokens, enforces discreteness and reuse through VQ commitment and diversity losses, and then builds compositional reasoning on top of these discrete atoms. The causal pathway from "continuous and entangled representations" to the solution is more direct in Method B, which intervenes at the representation-learning stage rather than only at the output/interface stage.

Method A's strongest novel element is the counterfactual return decomposition using Shapley-style/leave-one-out estimators applied to skill-level credit assignment, where a learned world model simulates counterfactual trajectories swapping chosen subgoals with alternatives to attribute marginal contributions. This is a non-trivial combination of cooperative game theory credit attribution with hierarchical RL option boundaries. Method B's strongest novel element is the multi-resolution credit mechanism with auxiliary value heads V_k at every plan depth k, trained to predict end-task return, combined with an explicit abstract progress state Z maintained by a learned state-writer. While multi-resolution/auxiliary objectives exist in prior work, the combination with a persistent progress state and search-based planning over (skill, parameter) tuples is moderately novel. Comparing the two: Method A's counterfactual Shapley-style attribution applied to hierarchical option selection is a more distinctly original mechanism that would be harder to find in existing literature, whereas Method B's multi-resolution auxiliary heads and progress state, while useful, are closer to combinations of known techniques (auxiliary tasks, working memory modules, HER, weighted A*).

The failure mode is credit assignment over long horizons in hierarchical tasks—specifically, how to attribute end-task rewards back to intermediate decisions (subgoal selections) that occurred many steps earlier. Method A addresses this through counterfactual return decomposition in Phase 3: it explicitly estimates each skill's marginal contribution by simulating counterfactual trajectories and using Shapley-style leave-one-out attribution as training targets for the meta-controller. This is a direct, traceable mechanism for solving long-horizon credit assignment. Method B addresses credit assignment through "multi-resolution auxiliary objectives"—attaching value heads V_k at each plan depth trained to predict end-task return, and backpropagating a weighted sum of losses over depths. This also directly targets the credit assignment problem by making intermediate states predictive of final outcomes. Both methods engage the failure mode directly, but Method B's multi-resolution credit mechanism is more tightly integrated throughout the entire planning and learning stack (Stages B, C, and D all reinforce it), while Method A's counterfactual decomposition is confined to Phase 3 and is more computationally speculative. However, Method A's mechanism is more causally precise—it directly estimates the contribution of each hierarchical decision to the final return, which is the exact definition of the credit assignment problem. Method B's auxiliary value heads reduce the temporal gap but don't decompose credit to specific decisions as precisely.

Method A's strongest novel element is the multi-resolution credit propagation mechanism (auxiliary value heads V_k at each plan depth trained to predict end-task return), combined with an explicit abstract progress state Z maintained by a learned state-writer, and a GNN-based skill-effect model used in look-ahead search. The combination of these three components—especially the multi-depth auxiliary objectives for credit assignment within a hierarchical planner—is not a standard published combination. Method B's strongest novel element is the dual change-point detection combining an information-bottleneck probe with a keyframe selector, intersected to discover subgoals, plus a "pseudo-interactive demonstrator" that uses the learned subgoal graph to propose corrective labels during rollouts. While the information-bottleneck for subgoal discovery has some precedent, the pseudo-demonstrator concept has some novelty. Comparing the two: Method A's multi-resolution credit mechanism with learned progress state and look-ahead planning represents a more tightly integrated and less precedented combination than Method B's ensemble of individually recognizable components (IB-based subgoal discovery, options with shaping, Bayesian belief tracking, pseudo-demonstration). Method A's core novelty—multi-resolution auxiliary value heads tied to plan depth within a hierarchical RL/planning stack—is harder to find in prior literature as a unified mechanism.

The failure mode is credit assignment over long horizons in tasks with nested goal structure, where flat policies struggle because rewards are sparse and temporally distant from the decisions that caused them. Method A addresses this through offline subgoal discovery, shaped reward potentials, and pseudo-demonstration labels that provide dense intermediate signals, but its credit assignment mechanism is somewhat indirect—it relies on shaping potentials and imitation of pseudo-labels rather than explicitly propagating end-task credit back through the hierarchy. Method B directly targets the credit assignment failure mode with its "multi-resolution credit" mechanism (Stage C): auxiliary value heads V_k at every plan depth are trained to predict end-task return, and a weighted sum of losses across depths is backpropagated, creating a traceable causal pathway from the long-horizon credit problem to a specific architectural step. Additionally, the explicit abstract progress state Z (Stage B) maintains consistent credit assignment to decisions that advanced subgoals. This is a more direct mechanical engagement with the named failure mode of credit assignment over long horizons.

Method A's strongest novel element is the multi-resolution credit propagation via auxiliary value heads at every plan depth (V_k), combined with a learned abstract progress state (state-writer) and GNN-based skill-effect models in a unified three-layer stack. While each component has precedents (HER, HRL, GNNs, A* planning), the specific combination of depth-indexed auxiliary value heads for "multi-resolution credit" within a hierarchical look-ahead planner is a reasonably original framing. Method B's strongest novel element is the state-dependent Lagrange multiplier for adaptive guidance between hierarchy levels, combined with automaton-compiled LTL specifications grounding skill discovery. However, automaton/LTL-based HRL is well-established (e.g., reward machines, LTL2Action), decoupled optimization of hierarchy levels is known, and SVGD particle ensembles for planners are borrowed from existing work. Method B is largely an engineering combination of known techniques. Method A's multi-resolution credit assignment mechanism—attaching auxiliary value heads at each planning depth and backpropagating a weighted sum—is a more distinctly novel framing not easily found in prior HRL literature, even if its components are individually known.

The failure mode is credit assignment over long horizons in complex sequential tasks with nested goal structure, and the query asks how to learn or impose a hierarchy that decomposes the task and accelerates learning. Method A addresses this through multi-resolution auxiliary value heads at every plan depth that directly backpropagate end-task credit to intermediate decisions, combined with a hierarchical skill library and abstract progress state—the causal pathway from long-horizon credit assignment to the multi-depth V_k heads is explicit. Method B addresses the failure mode by imposing an automaton-based temporal scaffold that provides dense shaped rewards at subgoal transitions, decoupling high-level and low-level optimization to prevent gradient dilution, and using a separate planner trained with subgoal-completeness rewards. Both methods directly engage the credit assignment failure mode, but Method A's mechanism is more tightly targeted: the multi-resolution credit propagation explicitly names and resolves the credit assignment problem at every intermediate abstract state, while Method B's decoupled optimization and automaton rewards address it more indirectly by separating levels and providing shaped rewards rather than directly propagating end-task credit through the hierarchy.

Method A's strongest novel element is the explicit Proposer-Verifier-Executor triad with differentiable constraint checking (geometric consistency, propensity-weighted counterfactual plausibility, etc.) and an edit-based refinement loop where the Proposer revises scratchpads conditioned on Verifier feedback. Method B's strongest novel element is the shared operator library (decompose/gather/verify/commit) with a faithfulness regularizer that enforces agreement between executed checks and the model's stated reasoning, plus backward-chaining from goals to visual subgoals. Both methods are fairly similar in their core framing—inserting structured intermediate computation across modalities with a verification step—and both draw on well-established ideas (scratchpads, world models, subgoal hierarchies, listwise reranking). Method A's verifier-gated edit-refinement loop and the domain-specific differentiable constraint residuals are somewhat more mechanistically specific and harder to find in prior literature as a unified system, while Method B's operator library and faithfulness regularizer are conceptually cleaner but closer to existing program-of-thought and process-reward model literature. The distinction is marginal, but Method A's combination of sample-and-verify with iterative scratchpad editing and domain-specific constraint residuals represents a slightly more novel synthesis.

The failure mode identified in the query is that models in vision, RL, and recommendation lack intermediate computation steps analogous to chain-of-thought reasoning in LLMs, which limits their accuracy. Both methods directly address this by proposing explicit intermediate computation frameworks across all three domains. Method A introduces a Proposer-Verifier-Executor scaffold with domain-specific scratchpads, while Method B introduces a shared control loop with decompose/gather/verify/commit operators. Both trace a clear causal pathway from the problem (no intermediate steps) to the solution (structured intermediates). However, Method A more explicitly connects the analogy to LLM chain-of-thought reasoning through its "scratchpad" framing and provides more detailed mechanistic descriptions of how intermediate artifacts are generated, verified, and used in each domain. Method B's control loop is also well-specified but relies more on teacher trajectories and cold-start supervision, which slightly shifts focus toward training methodology rather than the core mechanism of intermediate computation. Both are comparably direct in engaging the failure mode, with Method A's verifier-gated scratchpad being slightly more traceable as an analog to reasoning steps.

Method A's strongest novel element is the unified "Intermediate Computation Programs" framework with a named operator library (decompose/gather/verify/commit) and a faithfulness regularizer that explicitly measures agreement between executed checks and the model's narrated reasoning steps — the latter being a direct analog of chain-of-thought faithfulness verification applied cross-domain. Method B's strongest novel element is the confidence controller / uncertainty gate that adaptively allocates computation depth based on difficulty, combined with the Bradley-Terry pairwise preference graph in recommendation. However, adaptive computation depth via uncertainty gating is a well-established idea (e.g., early exit networks, adaptive computation time), and the Bradley-Terry ranking model is a classical approach. Method A's faithfulness regularizer — penalizing divergence between executed intermediate checks and stated reasoning — is a more original mechanism that does not have a clear direct precedent in the cross-domain intermediate computation literature, and the explicit operator library framing as a "control loop" with checkable state sketches is a more distinctive architectural contribution than CoComp's three-block recurrent structure, which closely mirrors existing encoder-proposal-decoder pipelines.

The failure mode identified in the query is the absence of intermediate computation steps in non-language models (vision, RL, recommendation), analogous to chain-of-thought in LLMs. Both methods directly engage this failure mode by proposing explicit intermediate computation frameworks across the same three domains. Method A uses a decompose/gather/verify/commit control loop with faithfulness regularization that enforces agreement between intermediate steps and final outputs, creating a traceable causal pathway from the LLM reasoning analogy to specific checkable intermediate states. Method B uses a three-block Evidence Builder/Local Proposal/Finalizer pattern with an uncertainty gate, also directly instantiated across all three domains. Both methods hit the failure mode head-on with comparable directness; however, Method A's explicit faithfulness regularizer and verifier mechanism more directly addresses the core concern of ensuring intermediates genuinely contribute to (rather than bypass) the final answer, which is the key challenge when transplanting chain-of-thought reasoning to non-language domains. Method B's uncertainty gate and consistency losses are somewhat more peripheral to this core concern.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of a verifier-in-the-loop with a per-step information-gain regularizer (supervisor predicting final outcome from scratchpad) and twist-guided Sequential Monte Carlo inference across three domains — the IG regularizer as a training signal for intermediate steps is a relatively distinctive mechanism. Method B's strongest novel element is the structured decompose/gather/verify/commit operator library with a faithfulness regularizer that enforces agreement between executed checks and narrated reasoning, plus the ordering-only feedback loop from reranker to retriever query embedding in recommendation. Both methods are largely engineering assemblies of known components (chain-of-thought analogues, options/subgoals, KG path reasoning, SMC, DPO) applied across domains. Method A's twist-guided SMC with verifier resampling and the information-gain regularizer as a training signal is a somewhat more distinctive combination than Method B's operator library with faithfulness regularizer, though neither is strongly novel. The IG regularizer tied to a supervisor predicting final outcomes from intermediate scratchpad states is the marginally more original mechanism compared to Method B's conflict-resolving verifier and faithfulness term, which are closer to existing self-consistency and rationale-grounding ideas.

The failure mode identified in the query is that models in vision, RL, and recommendation lack analogous intermediate computation steps to chain-of-thought reasoning in LLMs, which limits their accuracy. Both methods directly address this by proposing structured intermediate computation frameworks across all three domains. Method A (IPC) traces a causal pathway through a scratchpad/reasoning graph, verifier-guided step rewards, and domain-specific instantiations with explicit intermediate steps. Method B (ICP) similarly traces a pathway through decompose/gather/verify/commit operators, cold-start supervision, and faithfulness regularizers. Both engage the failure mode head-on with comparable directness: each proposes a shared control loop with explicit intermediate states, domain-specific instantiations, and training procedures that reward intermediate step quality. The mechanisms are structurally similar, and neither is meaningfully more direct than the other in resolving the named failure mode.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of a utility-predictor with TTL-based eviction, multi-scale span summarization with a background "retrospective compressor," and a retrieval-contrastive objective using gradient-attribution delayed labels to align memory writes with future loss reduction. The lookahead query mechanism (predicting next-token features to probe memory) is also a relatively original training signal. Method B's strongest novel element is the uncertainty-weighted exponential moving average for memory updates combined with ego-motion-warped deformable attention for streaming video, plus the explicit symbolic world-state store with a query-conditioned router that selects across tiers. Both methods combine multiple known components (external memory, sparse attention, ANN retrieval, auxiliary losses), but Method A introduces more distinctly novel training signals—particularly the gradient-attribution-based retrieval-contrastive objective and the retrospective compressor—that are harder to find in prior literature as a combined mechanism. Method B's components (deformable attention, EMA updates, slot tokens, symbolic stores) are individually more established in prior work.

The failure mode is quadratic attention cost combined with forgetting of distant information in long contexts. Both methods directly address this by replacing full-context attention with bounded memory systems. Method A uses a four-tier memory (local window, streaming feature map, sparse core-token buffer, symbolic world-state) with query-conditioned retrieval, explicitly bounding compute to fixed budgets independent of history length. Method B uses hierarchical multi-scale summaries with a budgeted eviction policy and two-stage retrieval, also achieving near-linear compute. Both trace a clear causal pathway from the problem (quadratic cost + forgetting) to specific mechanisms (bounded attention contexts, selective memory writes, retrieval-based reads). However, Method A provides more explicit and traceable mechanisms for each component of the failure mode: the positional structure preservation and training-inference mismatch fix directly address forgetting, while the tier-based routing with fixed budgets L, S, T directly addresses compute scaling. Method B's mechanisms are comparably direct but rely more on learned utility predictors and eviction policies whose causal pathways to the failure mode are slightly less precisely specified.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of gradient-based attribution for online core-token selection, ego-motion-warped deformable attention over streaming feature maps, and an explicit symbolic world-state reader/writer — particularly the gradient-based relevance scoring for token selection during streaming ingestion, which is an unusual mechanism for memory curation. Method B's strongest novel element is the continuous-time latent state that evolves between discrete event writes, combined with a prototype-based novelty detector and progress-score diversity constraint ensuring phase coverage, plus pseudo-relevance feedback query refinement. Both methods are elaborate multi-component systems, but Method B introduces a more distinctly named and framed mechanism: the continuous-time latent state bridging discrete event writes (borrowed from neural ODEs/SSMs but applied here in a novel event-memory context) combined with the progress-score phase-coverage constraint is a combination not easily found in prior retrieval-augmented attention literature. Method A's components, while well-integrated, are closer to combinations of known ideas (deformable attention, EMA updates, ANN retrieval, symbolic stores). The continuous-time latent + phase-diversity constraint in Method B represents a slightly more original framing for this specific problem.

The failure mode is quadratic attention cost and forgetting of distant information in long contexts. Both methods address this by replacing full-context attention with bounded, sparse attention over curated memory. Method A uses a continuous-time latent state plus sparse event memory with dual-scale attention (local window + cross-attention to retrieved events), directly reducing O(T²) to O(W²+Wk+k²). Method B uses a four-tier system (local window, streaming feature map, core-token buffer, world-state store) with query-conditioned retrieval and bounded attention. Both directly engage the quadratic cost problem through sparse/bounded attention and the forgetting problem through structured memory. However, Method A provides a more traceable causal pathway: it explicitly names the complexity reduction formula, explains how the continuous-time latent bridges between discrete events to avoid re-attending to every token, and details how the contrastive training objective ensures distant memories remain retrievable. Method B's mechanism is comparably direct but spreads across four tiers with more peripheral components (ego-motion warping, zero-padding for positional structure) that address adjacent concerns rather than the core failure mode.

Method A's strongest novel element is the MaxLoss-guided consolidation policy combined with self-synthesized rehearsal (regenerating distilled summaries via the current model to keep memory slots aligned with evolving representations) and the skill/task descriptor rewriting for retrieval with a predictor-gated slot filter. These are relatively incremental combinations of known ideas (RWKV recurrence, token pruning, replay-based continual learning, retrieval-augmented generation). Method B's strongest novel element is the uncertainty-weighted exponential moving average for memory updates (where surprising observations adjust memory more aggressively), combined with gradient-based attribution for online core-token selection, ego-motion-warped deformable attention over streaming feature maps, and an explicit symbolic world-state store with a query-routing tier selector. The combination of ego-motion warping with deformable attention for long-horizon video memory, gradient-based online token selection, and the four-tier architecture with explicit symbolic world state represents a more distinctive and harder-to-find combination in the literature than Method A's RWKV + replay + retrieval stack.

The failure mode is quadratic attention cost and forgetting of distant information in long contexts. Both methods directly engage this failure mode by replacing full-context attention with bounded memory structures. Method A addresses it through RWKV-style linear recurrence for local processing, dual-tier memory with slot budgets, and sparse retrieval at query time—each step traceable to reducing quadratic cost and preserving distant information. Method B addresses it through four cooperating tiers (local window, streaming feature map, sparse core-token buffer, world-state store), with attention performed only over a bounded assembled set rather than full history, plus gradient-based token selection and positional dropout training to handle partial contexts. Both methods are comparably direct: both replace quadratic attention with bounded-context attention, both maintain compressed representations of distant information, and both have traceable causal pathways from the problem to specific architectural steps. Method B's explicit handling of the training-inference mismatch (via token dropout matching retention rate) and its multi-modal tiering (warped streaming maps for video, slot tokens for text) are slightly more concrete in addressing the "forgetting" aspect across modalities, but Method A's RWKV backbone more directly eliminates quadratic cost at the token level. The directness of engagement is comparable across both methods.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of a salience cache (offline precomputed branch-usage statistics used as a lookup table refined by a tiny MLP) with Matryoshka-style nested slice parameterization applied jointly to branches and layers, plus cosine-similarity-based early exit with depth-decayed thresholds. The salience cache initialization for routing is a relatively uncommon mechanism. Method B's strongest novel element is the hierarchical router with uncertainty-aware routing that adapts k dynamically and falls back to a default expert, combined with two-phase training using Gumbel-Softmax hardening. However, Method B's components—MoE with top-k routing, load balancing, knowledge distillation, Gumbel-Softmax, capacity constraints, and early exit—are all well-established individually and in combination. Method A introduces more genuinely non-standard combinations: the offline salience cache for routing initialization, branch dropout with full-branch supervision targets, and Matryoshka slicing applied within branches are less standard combinations that are harder to find in prior literature as a unified system.

The failure mode is dense models computing every parameter for every input, wasting compute when only a fraction is needed. Method A addresses this through multiple mechanisms: branch routing (top-k branch selection per block), Matryoshka-style nested capacity (activating only needed parameter slices), dynamic early exiting (skipping entire downstream layers), and salience-cached routing (cheap gating). Each mechanism has a traceable causal pathway to the core problem of conditional subnetwork activation. Method B addresses the same failure mode through a Mixture-of-Experts architecture with a hierarchical router that activates only top-k experts per token per layer, with hard routing at inference and capacity constraints. Both methods directly engage the failure mode of conditional activation. However, Method A's mechanisms are more comprehensively and directly tied to the specific challenge of activating "only the relevant subnetwork" - it addresses this at multiple granularities (layer-level via early exit, block-level via branch routing, and parameter-level via Matryoshka slicing), while Method B primarily addresses it at the expert level with additional engineering concerns. Method B's description spends considerable space on engineering/deployment details (kernel fusion, telemetry, hardware mapping) that are peripheral to the core validity question.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of an offline salience cache (precomputed branch-usage statistics used as a lookup table to initialize routing) with Matryoshka-style nested slice parameterization applied jointly to branches and layers, plus branch dropout to prevent co-adaptation. The salience cache initialization is a relatively distinctive mechanism not commonly seen in conditional computation literature. Method B's strongest novel element is the factored shared/domain-specific expert decomposition with a pre-router trained via compute-aware distillation, combined with uncertainty-driven elastic activation. However, each of these components (hierarchical pre-routing, MoE with load balancing, domain-factored experts, early exit with uncertainty, Gumbel-Softmax relaxation) is well-established in prior literature and the combination, while competent, follows a fairly predictable assembly of known techniques. Method A's offline salience cache for routing initialization and the Matryoshka nested-slice parameterization applied to branch-level routing represents a more unusual combination that is harder to find in existing literature as a unified system.

The failure mode is dense models computing every parameter for every input, wasting compute when only a fraction of the network is needed. Both methods address conditional activation, but differ in directness. Method A proposes branch routing with early exit, Matryoshka-style nested subnetworks, and salience-cached routing—all mechanisms that directly gate which branches/layers/slices are activated per input, with traceable pathways from the problem (wasted compute) to specific steps (branch dropout, top-k routing, early exit thresholds). Method B proposes a hierarchical pre-router, sparse MoE with per-block expert pruning, domain factoring, and uncertainty-based escalation—also directly addressing conditional activation, with traceable pathways from the problem to each component. Both methods engage the failure mode head-on with comparable directness: both use input-dependent routing to activate only relevant subnetworks, both include early-exit mechanisms, and both include load-balancing to prevent collapse. The mechanisms are similarly comprehensive and similarly traceable to the core problem.

Method A's strongest novel element is the "twist" lookahead head that predicts expected downstream loss reduction per token to adaptively modulate gate thresholds across depth — this input-and-depth-aware controller that learns from toggling candidate subsets is a relatively original training signal for conditional computation. Method B's strongest novel element is the combination of Matryoshka-style nested slice parameterization with parallel branch dropout and early-exit via hidden-state cosine similarity saturation detection — the Matryoshka approach applied to branches/heads for nested subnetwork routing is notable, but Matryoshka representations are an established concept (Kusupati et al.), and branch dropout with load balancing is a known MoE technique. The offline salience cache initialization is a practical engineering contribution but not deeply novel. Comparing the two: Method A's "twist" mechanism — a per-block lookahead value head trained on downstream loss improvement from toggling subsets — is a more original and harder-to-find combination than Method B's assembly of existing techniques (Matryoshka nesting, branch dropout, early exit via cosine similarity), even though Method B's overall system is more architecturally diverse.

The failure mode is dense models computing every parameter for every input, wasting compute when only a fraction of the network is needed. Method B (CoSA) directly addresses this by predicting which specific FFN neurons and attention heads are actually needed per token, then performing matmuls only for those selected units—this is a direct, traceable causal pathway from "wasting compute on unused parameters" to "skip the matmuls for unselected neurons/heads." Method A (SDSR) also addresses conditional activation but does so through branch routing, early exits, and Matryoshka slicing—mechanisms that are more about selecting among pre-defined parallel branches and nested capacity levels rather than directly identifying and skipping the specific subnetwork of neurons/heads that are relevant to a given input. Method B's mechanism of predicting active neurons/heads per token more directly maps to the query's framing of "conditionally activating only the relevant subnetwork," as it operates at the granularity of individual neurons and heads rather than coarser branch structures.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of Matryoshka nested sub-models with submodular capability source selection and listwise ranking distillation — the Matryoshka aspect is a known technique (Kusupati et al. 2022) applied here in a distillation context, and submodular selection for knowledge distillation is a reasonable but not unprecedented extension. Method B's strongest novel element is the explicit "capability-use vector" — a structured schema elicited from the teacher per instance indicating which micro-capabilities were invoked — combined with contrast sets that toggle individual capabilities to validate disentanglement, and an active teaching loop that targets specific capability gaps. The capability-use vector as a supervisory signal with contrastive capability-toggling pairs is a more original framing: it introduces a new intermediate representation (the capability code) that is explicitly disentangled and validated, which is harder to find in prior distillation literature. Method A's combination, while elaborate, assembles more recognizable components (Matryoshka networks, submodular selection, attention-based attribution, listwise ranking loss). Method B's capability-sliced approach with contrast-set-validated disentanglement is the more original mechanism.

The failure mode is that smaller specialists underperform even on their own domain because they lack the relevant generalist capabilities, and the challenge is selectively transferring only the domain-relevant subset of the generalist's competence without the cost of the full model. Method A addresses this through capability discovery/selection and structured distillation, but its mechanism is heavily weighted toward the Matryoshka multi-width deployment architecture and listwise ranking signals—these are useful but somewhat peripheral to the core problem of identifying and transferring the *specific* generalist capabilities that matter for the domain. Method B directly engages the failure mode by explicitly defining micro-capabilities, constructing contrast sets that isolate individual capabilities, learning a low-dimensional capability code that captures only the domain-relevant generalist subspace, and then distilling specifically into adapters conditioned on that code with contrastive losses enforcing that each adapter dimension controls the intended behavior. The causal pathway from "generalist has relevant capabilities the specialist lacks" to "identify those capabilities, extract their subspace, transfer via targeted adapters" is more traceable and direct in Method B.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of preference-aligned (Bradley–Terry pairwise) distillation with uncertainty-gated DAgger-style data aggregation and a factorized latent (z_shared/z_dom) with adversarial alignment — a multi-component system, but each piece (DAgger, preference distillation, adversarial domain adaptation, factorized representations) exists independently in prior literature, and their combination, while useful, is not a single striking new mechanism. Method B's strongest novel element is the Matryoshka student architecture applied specifically to knowledge distillation from a generalist, combined with submodular capability discovery/selection from teacher sources and instance-specific evidential focus masks for representation distillation. The Matryoshka representation learning concept exists (Kusupati et al.), but applying it as a "train-once, deploy-small" nested student for selective capability distillation — combined with submodular teacher source selection — is a more distinctive and less obvious combination than Method A's assemblage. The submodular transferability-and-diversity scoring of teacher components is a particularly original framing not commonly seen in distillation literature.

The failure mode is that small specialist models underperform even on their own domain because they lack the relevant generalist capabilities, and the challenge is transferring only those relevant capabilities efficiently. Both methods address this directly: Method A uses capability discovery/selection to identify transferable teacher components, task-conditioned supervision with listwise signals, and Matryoshka subspaces for flexible deployment. Method B uses representation transfer with factorized latents, preference-aligned distillation, and uncertainty-gated DAgger for iterative refinement. Both hit the core failure mode (selective transfer of relevant generalist capability to a small model). However, Method B more directly addresses the "worse on the specialist's own domain" aspect through its DAgger-style data aggregation that explicitly trains the student on its own deployment distribution—a known cause of specialist underperformance—and its uncertainty-gated querying that targets exactly the cases where the small model fails. Method A's Matryoshka component, while useful for deployment flexibility, is somewhat peripheral to the core transfer problem. Both methods are comparably direct on the main transfer mechanism, making this essentially a tie, but Method B's DAgger component provides a more traceable causal pathway from the specific failure mode (small model underperforms on its own domain) to a corrective mechanism.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of zeroth-order (forward-pass-only) optimization in a low-dimensional subspace for adapter training, which avoids backpropagation and fits inference memory budgets. While interesting, zeroth-order fine-tuning (e.g., MeZO) and parameter-efficient adapters are established components, and their combination here is incremental. Method B's strongest novel element is the Matryoshka student architecture combined with listwise preference distillation and instance-specific relevance masking (attribution-guided representation distillation). The Matryoshka network for distillation (train-once, deploy at multiple widths) combined with listwise ranking supervision from the teacher and submodular capability selection is a more original combination—particularly the integration of Matryoshka nested subspaces with capability-selective distillation as a unified framework. Neither method is entirely unprecedented, but Method B's combination of submodular source selection, listwise teacher supervision, attribution-masked representation distillation, and Matryoshka multi-width students is harder to find as a unified prior work.

The failure mode is that smaller specialist models lack the generalist's broad capabilities in the specialist's own domain, and the challenge is transferring only the relevant generalist capability efficiently. Both methods address this through teacher-student distillation with capability-focused filtering and parameter-efficient adaptation. Method A directly traces a causal pathway: it scopes target capabilities via skill-centric retrieval, labels a curated pool with the generalist, uses confidence-weighted distillation into a lightweight adapter on the frozen specialist, and adds a fallback mechanism—each step directly addresses the transfer problem. Method B introduces the Matryoshka nested sub-model structure as a central pillar, which primarily addresses deployment flexibility across latency budgets rather than the core failure mode of transferring generalist capability to a specialist. While Method B's capability discovery and task-conditioned supervision steps do engage the failure mode, the Matryoshka component is a peripheral concern relative to the stated problem. Method A's mechanism more directly and completely traces from the identified failure mode to each method step.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of a learned latent space with explicitly discovered label-preserving (nuisance) axes, adversarial inner-loop optimization over those axes, and test-time ensembling over latent counterfactuals — forming a principled "latent adversarial counterfactual" framework. The nuisance-axis discovery procedure and the certified robustness via randomized smoothing in latent space is a relatively original combination not commonly found together in domain generalization literature. Method B's strongest novel element is the domain-aware blending of direct features with prototype-enhanced features weighted by a learned domain score, combined with a non-parametric FIFO-memory correction loop that estimates pseudo-error vectors at test time. While the individual components (prototypes, influence reweighting, confidence calibration) are well-established, the pseudo-error correction loop has some novelty. However, Method A's core mechanism — adversarially training against worst-case perturbations along latent nuisance axes discovered without target data, with probabilistic certificates — represents a more original and harder-to-find combination than Method B's assembly of largely known components with incremental connections.

The failure mode is that test-time distributions shift along axes the model never saw during training, and the challenge is achieving robustness without observing the target distribution. Method B directly engages this failure mode by explicitly constructing a latent space that spans axes of variation beyond the supervised source data, identifying label-preserving nuisance directions, and adversarially training against worst-case perturbations along those unseen axes—the causal pathway from "unseen shift axes" to "latent traversal along nuisance directions" is traceable and direct. Method A addresses distribution shift through source-side regularization (influence reweighting, semantic consistency, prototype memory, confidence calibration), but its mechanism primarily tightens robustness within the source distribution's support rather than explicitly constructing or training against axes that were never observed; the domain indicator and prototype correction help at test time but do not mechanistically address the "never-seen axes" aspect of the failure mode. Method B's core mechanism—discovering and adversarially training over label-preserving latent directions that extend beyond observed variation—more directly targets the named failure mode.

Method A's strongest novel element is the non-parametric, FIFO-memory-based pseudo-error correction loop that estimates residuals between direct features and prototype-enhanced features from nearest neighbors, then applies a clamped correction at test time — this is a somewhat unusual inference-time mechanism, though it combines well-known components (prototype memory, nearest-neighbor retrieval, residual correction). Method B's strongest novel element is the explicit causal-invariant partitioning with group-dependent variational priors over z_env and shared priors over z_inv, combined with uncertainty-gated prompt adaptation that uses a cache of prompt states keyed by inferred nuisance signatures (z_env clusters) for fast reuse — the prompt-state cache keyed by environment codes is a relatively distinctive combination. Both methods are assemblages of known techniques, but Method B introduces a more coherent and less precedented combination: causal disentanglement with group-dependent variational priors feeding into a prompt-state cache indexed by environment codes is harder to find as a unified prior work than Method A's prototype-memory correction loop, which closely resembles existing test-time adaptation and memory-augmented inference literature.

The failure mode is distributional shift along axes never seen during training, requiring robustness without target distribution access. Method A addresses this through causal invariant representation learning (separating task-relevant from nuisance factors) combined with test-time unsupervised prompt adaptation—directly targeting both the "unseen axes" problem (via invariant partitioning) and the "no target distribution" constraint (via label-free adaptation). The causal pathway is clear: invariant representations resist known shifts, while uncertainty-gated prompt tuning handles genuinely novel axes at test time without labels. Method B addresses the failure mode through probabilistic reweighting, semantic consistency constraints, and a prototype-based correction loop—these mechanisms primarily improve robustness within the source distribution's variation and rely on prototype-enhanced features that are anchored to source class structure, which may not generalize to truly unseen axes. Method A's explicit mechanism for handling novel, unseen axes (the uncertainty-gated, label-free prompt adaptation combined with causal partitioning) more directly engages the core failure mode of shifts along axes never observed during training.

Method A's strongest novel element is the training-free, non-parametric correction loop using a FIFO memory of hidden states and pseudo-error vectors derived from prototype residuals, combined with a domain-aware blending mechanism gated by a source-trained domain score head. While interesting, each component (prototype memory, nearest-neighbor retrieval, confidence calibration) has clear precedents in test-time adaptation and prototype network literature. The combination is incremental rather than fundamentally new.

Method B's strongest novel element is the "robustness amplification vectors" mechanism—computing axis-specific delta vectors by contrasting clean vs. transformed activations offline, then injecting linear combinations of these vectors at inference time to steer representations back toward an invariant manifold. This activation-steering approach for distribution shift robustness, combined with the coverage-driven envelope construction using orthogonal clusters in embedding space and the "union" operator for fusing next-token probabilities, represents a more distinctive framing. The self-synthesized rehearsal mechanism (prompting the base model to recreate prior examples for replay) is also a relatively novel continual learning component. Overall, Method B introduces more mechanisms that are harder to find as a combined system in prior literature.

The failure mode is distributional shift along axes the model never encountered during training, without access to the target distribution. Method A directly addresses this by (a) building a coverage-driven training set using diverse shift operators to approximate an envelope around unseen axes, (b) training with invariance-centered objectives that explicitly target multiple shift dimensions, and (c) deploying robustness amplification vectors computed per shift axis to steer representations back toward an invariant manifold at inference—each step has a traceable causal pathway from the named failure mode. Method B addresses the problem through source-distribution reweighting, semantic consistency constraints, prototype-enhanced blending, and a non-parametric correction loop, but its mechanisms are more oriented toward handling known source submode variation and style cues rather than explicitly covering unseen shift axes; the prototype memory and domain indicator are trained on source data with generic augmentations, which provides less direct coverage of novel axes. Method A's explicit axis-enumeration and coverage-driven envelope construction more directly targets the "axes the model never saw" aspect of the failure mode.

Method A's strongest novel element is the calibrated KL-range controller that tunes edit aggressiveness per-feature until the model's logit distribution reaches a target divergence range, combined with iterative shortcut re-estimation after training phases. However, concept-level shortcut discovery, counterfactual consistency training, and IRM/group-DRO are all well-established components assembled here in a relatively straightforward pipeline. Method B's strongest novel element is the unsupervised disentanglement of content and nuisance encoders with opposing probe objectives (environment probe maximized on s, minimized on c; label probe maximized on c, minimized on s), followed by cross-environment nuisance swapping via a decoder to synthesize counterfactuals—this content/nuisance disentanglement-then-swap mechanism as a counterfactual generation strategy is a more distinctive architectural contribution. While disentangled representations and environment-based invariance learning exist separately in the literature, the specific combination of annotation-free environment discovery through nuisance encoder clustering, opposing probe disentanglement, and decoder-based nuisance swapping as the counterfactual generation mechanism is a more original framing than Method A's pipeline of established components with a calibration controller added.

The failure mode is models learning spurious correlations (shortcuts) tied to non-causal features, which fail under intervention. The core challenge is: (a) identifying which features are non-causal shortcuts, (b) generating counterfactuals that break those shortcuts while preserving labels, and (c) training for invariance. Method A directly targets this by explicitly measuring shortcut risk via concept-bias scores and feature dependency measures, then generating calibrated counterfactuals with a KL-controlled edit strength to ensure the shortcut is broken without label drift, and finally training with consistency losses plus group-robust objectives. The causal pathway from problem to method step is clear and traceable at each stage. Method B also addresses the failure mode but routes through a disentanglement framework (content/nuisance encoders) that must first learn to separate causal from non-causal factors before generating counterfactuals—this introduces an additional assumption that the disentanglement succeeds, which is itself a non-trivial problem. Method A's shortcut-discovery mechanism more directly measures the model's reliance on specific features (via prediction shift under single-variable interventions), making the engagement with the failure mode more direct and traceable, whereas Method B's environment-clustering approach is more indirect and relies on unsupervised structure that may not align with actual shortcuts.

Method A's strongest novel element is the split latent space (z_causal, z_env) with adversarial GRL disentanglement, pseudo-environment clustering, DAgger-style interactive proposer for targeted z_env swaps, and a scaffold-to-free curriculum over counterfactual severity with information-bottleneck monitoring. The combination of iterative pseudo-environment discovery with a DAgger-style adversarial proposer and severity curriculum is a reasonably original mechanism. Method B's strongest novel element is the concept-level shortcut vocabulary built via LLM annotations combined with a KL-calibrated edit-strength controller that tunes perturbation aggressiveness to hit a target divergence range, plus mining natural counterfactual pairs with interventional-pairing weighting. The KL-range controller for calibrating edit strength is a practical but relatively straightforward idea, and the concept-bias/dependency scoring combines known techniques. Method A introduces a more architecturally novel combination—the disentangled latent space with adversarial pseudo-environment induction, DAgger-style proposer, and curriculum—that is harder to find as a unified prior contribution, whereas Method B largely assembles well-established components (IRM/DRO, LLM editing, concept probing) with a calibration wrapper.

The failure mode is models relying on spurious correlations (shortcuts) that fail under intervention, and the challenge is generating counterfactuals that break these shortcuts and induce invariance to non-causal features. Method A addresses this by first discovering which features are non-causal shortcuts (via concept-bias scores and feature dependency measures), then generating minimally edited counterfactuals that toggle those features, and finally training with consistency constraints—creating a traceable causal pathway from shortcut identification to targeted intervention to invariance training. Method B addresses the same failure mode through a latent space factorization approach (z_causal vs z_env), swapping environment components while preserving causal components, and training with invariance constraints. Both methods directly engage the failure mode, but Method A's mechanism is more directly traceable: it explicitly identifies which features are shortcuts before generating counterfactuals, ensuring the edits target the actual failure mode. Method B's approach relies on the latent factorization correctly separating causal from non-causal factors, which introduces an additional assumption that may not hold, making the causal pathway slightly less direct.

Method A's strongest novel element is the calibrated KL-range controller that tunes edit aggressiveness until the model's pre-softmax distribution reaches a target divergence range, combined with iterative concept-bias/dependency re-estimation after each training phase. Method B's strongest novel element is the use of twisted Sequential Monte Carlo (twisted SMC) for counterfactual generation, where learned twists concentrate sampling on valid counterfactuals satisfying multiple simultaneous constraints (concept flip, semantic preservation, label preservation). Twisted SMC applied to counterfactual generation for shortcut-breaking is a genuinely unusual mechanism not commonly seen in this literature, whereas Method A's KL controller and iterative pipeline, while well-integrated, recombine more familiar components. The twisted SMC route in Method B represents a harder-to-find combination in prior work on counterfactual data augmentation.

The failure mode is models relying on spurious correlations (shortcuts) that fail under intervention, requiring counterfactual examples that break these shortcuts and induce invariance to non-causal features. Both methods address this failure mode with nearly identical three-stage pipelines: (1) shortcut discovery via concept-level annotation and bias scoring, (2) counterfactual generation that flips non-causal features while preserving labels, and (3) invariance training using counterfactual-pair consistency objectives. Both trace a direct causal pathway from the problem (spurious correlations → shortcut reliance) to specific method steps (discovery → generation → invariance training). Method A provides more mechanistic detail in its generation stage (three complementary routes with specific validity checks) and its invariance training (multiple loss terms), while Method B adds multi-modal coverage and natural intervention mining. However, both engage the failure mode with comparable directness—the core mechanism (discover shortcuts, generate counterfactuals that flip them, train for consistency) is structurally identical and equally traceable to the problem statement. Neither method's additional details represent a fundamentally more direct engagement with the named failure mode.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of a "fidelity score" (class stability across stochastic samples) with an online conformal correction using a single adaptive shrinkage parameter kappa — but each of these components (MC-dropout uncertainty, conformal calibration, online adaptation) is individually well-established, and their combination, while reasonable, does not introduce a clearly new mechanism or framing. Method B's strongest novel element is the conditional Dirichlet calibration with importance-weighted fitting using density-ratio weights from a shift discriminator, combined with a conformal prediction set layer that uses those same weights for finite-sample coverage guarantees under covariate shift. The use of a Dirichlet output (rather than scalar temperature or vector scaling) conditioned on shift-discriminator features and intermediate representations, optimized with importance-weighted proper scoring rules, represents a more original combination that is harder to find in existing calibration literature as a unified system. Both methods combine known components, but Method B's Dirichlet parameterization conditioned on density-ratio-weighted shift features is a more distinctive and less obvious combination than Method A's fidelity-augmented conformal wrapper.

The failure mode is overconfidence under distribution shift—models assign high probabilities to wrong predictions, and this miscalibration worsens when the test distribution differs from training. Method A addresses this through stochastic sampling for uncertainty/fidelity estimation plus an online conformal correction that adapts to shift via a sliding window of recent labeled predictions. However, its handling of distribution shift is primarily reactive (online correction) rather than explicitly modeling the source-to-target density ratio. Method B directly engages the distribution shift failure mode by estimating density-ratio weights p_T(x)/p_S(x) via a shift discriminator, then importance-weighting the calibration loss so the calibrator is explicitly trained to match the target distribution—this is a direct causal pathway from "distribution shift causes miscalibration" to "reweight calibration to match target." Method B also overlays conformal guarantees under covariate shift using these same weights. Both methods address calibration, but Method B's mechanism more directly targets the distribution shift component of the failure mode through explicit density-ratio estimation and importance weighting, while Method A's shift handling is more peripheral (online sliding window adaptation).

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of uncertainty-fidelity decomposition (separating task ambiguity from class commitment stability) with a Lipschitz-regularized calibrator and an online conformal correction using a single adaptive shrinkage parameter. The fidelity score concept (top-1 stability and margin across stochastic samples) is a somewhat distinctive framing, but the overall pipeline assembles well-known components: MC-dropout/TTA uncertainty, conformal prediction, and online threshold adaptation. Method B's strongest novel element is the Kalman-style state-space online adaptation of calibrator parameters, where process and measurement noise are estimated via exponential moving averages to track miscalibration under shift. This is a more distinctive mechanism not commonly seen in calibration literature. Additionally, the prototype-layer-derived latent-evidence embedding combined with input-conditional per-class temperature/bias prediction driven by shift scores represents a more architecturally integrated and less standard combination than Method A's pipeline. The Kalman filter adaptation for calibrator tracking is the clearest novel contribution distinguishing Method B.

The failure mode is overconfidence in deep models, particularly under distribution shift, requiring calibration that reflects actual likelihoods. Method A addresses this through stochastic sampling for uncertainty/fidelity estimation, a Lipschitz-regularized calibrator, and an online conformal correction that adapts to shift via a sliding window of recent labeled predictions. Method B addresses this through a prototype-based uncertainty score, an explicit shift detector (s(x)) that quantifies how far a test input is from the training distribution, input-conditional vector scaling that directly incorporates shift severity into per-class temperature adjustments, and a Kalman-style online adaptation of calibrator parameters under shift. Both methods engage the failure mode, but Method B more directly targets distribution shift by explicitly estimating shift severity as a continuous input to the calibration function and using it to modulate temperatures per class, while also providing a principled online adaptation mechanism (Kalman filter) that tracks miscalibration as the environment changes. Method A's conformal correction adapts online but requires a stream of labeled predictions and operates via a single shrinkage parameter rather than directly conditioning on shift magnitude. Method B's causal pathway from "distribution shift causes miscalibration" to "shift score s(x) modulates per-class temperature" is more traceable and direct.

Method A's strongest novel element is the two-path mixture-of-experts architecture with a learned OOD-score-dependent gate that explicitly routes between an ID calibrator and an OOD calibrator, combined with the black-box LLM-specific "behavioral fidelity" signal derived from content-preserving option substitutions (paraphrasing alternatives as negations). The density-ratio importance weighting to simulate OOD shift when only ID labels are available is also a non-trivial contribution. Method B's strongest novel element is the combination of Lipschitz-regularized calibration with online conformal correction using a single adaptive shrinkage parameter kappa — but Lipschitz-regularized calibrators and conformal prediction for calibration are both well-established ideas, and their combination here is relatively straightforward. The uncertainty-fidelity decomposition in Method B is similar to what appears in Method A and in prior MC-dropout/TTA literature. Method A's mixture-of-experts gating mechanism with learned OOD-aware routing and the behavioral fidelity probe for black-box LLMs represent a more original combination not easily found in prior calibration literature, whereas Method B assembles known components (Lipschitz regularization, conformal prediction, MC-dropout uncertainty) in a more conventional pipeline.

The failure mode is overconfidence under distribution shift—models assign high probabilities even when wrong, and this miscalibration worsens when the input distribution shifts. Both methods address calibration under shift, but they differ in how directly they engage the distribution-shift component. Method A explicitly builds a two-path mixture-of-experts with a dedicated OOD calibrator and a learned gate driven by an OOD score, creating a traceable causal pathway: OOD score → gate → blend of ID/OOD calibrators → shift-robust probability. It also simulates shift via importance weighting when only ID labels are available, directly targeting the distribution-shift failure mode. Method B addresses shift primarily through a single Lipschitz-regularized calibrator (which provides smoothness/robustness but does not explicitly model the shift) plus an online conformal correction that adapts a single scalar parameter. The conformal correction is reactive rather than proactively modeling the shift structure. Method A's explicit OOD-aware gating mechanism more directly resolves the named failure mode of calibration degrading under distribution shift.

Method A's strongest novel element is the bootstrapped pseudo-pair contrast with confidence-weighted soft targets combining momentum teacher, cycle-consistency, and prototype-level contrastive alignment through paired anchors — a reasonably original combination but built from well-known components (EMA teachers, cycle-consistency, prototypical contrastive learning). Method B's strongest novel element is the parametric copula modeling of cross-modal dependency: fitting Gaussian-mixture marginals over latent codes and learning a copula to model their joint distribution, optimized via a variational ELBO that incorporates both paired and unpaired data.
