Using copula-based distributional coupling as a contrastive alignment mechanism for cross-modal learning is a distinctly less common framing that would be harder to find in prior VLP literature. Additionally, Method B introduces a text-only LLM judge for scoring pseudo-pair quality without requiring gold pairs, and listwise ranking objectives for alignment refinement — each adding incremental novelty. The copula ELBO component in particular represents a more original mechanistic contribution than anything in Method A.

The failure mode is the scarcity of aligned text-image pairs, which makes standard cross-modal contrastive learning data-inefficient. Both methods address this by (a) using unimodal contrastive objectives on abundant unpaired data to structure each modality's representation space, (b) leveraging the scarce paired data for cross-modal alignment, and (c) bootstrapping pseudo-pairs from unpaired data to extend cross-modal supervision. Method A adds peripheral mechanisms (copula/GMM distributional coupling, listwise ranking objectives, LLM-based scoring judges) that, while potentially useful, are not directly tied to the core failure mode and dilute the causal pathway. Method B presents a cleaner, more direct three-phase pipeline—unimodal pretraining → anchored cross-modal contrast on scarce pairs → bootstrapped pseudo-pair contrast with confidence weighting and cycle-consistency—where each step has a traceable causal link to exploiting unimodal data for cross-modal learning. Both methods engage the failure mode comparably at their core, but Method B's mechanism is more tightly scoped and directly traceable to the problem, while Method A's additional components (copula ELBO, listwise curriculum, LLM judge) are peripheral elaborations that reduce clarity of the causal pathway.

Method A's strongest novel element is the copula-based distributional coupling of modality marginals — fitting Gaussian-mixture marginals and a parametric copula to model cross-modal dependency, with a variational ELBO that includes marginal likelihood terms on unpaired singletons. This is a genuinely unusual mechanism for cross-modal contrastive learning. Method B's strongest novel element is the "mix-and-separate" auxiliary objective — forming mini-batches of independently drawn texts and images and training a Hungarian-matched assignment head to recover correct 1-1 alignment through z_shared only. This is a creative self-supervised signal that doesn't require ground-truth pairs. Both methods share many common elements (EMA teachers, pseudo-pair mining, curriculum, unimodal self-supervision, pairwise ranking). The copula-based joint distribution modeling in Method A is a more distinctive and harder-to-find mechanism in the cross-modal contrastive learning literature than the mix-and-separate auxiliary, which resembles permutation-recovery objectives seen in other self-supervised contexts.

The failure mode is the scarcity of aligned text-image pairs, requiring a mechanism that directly exploits abundant unimodal data to generate cross-modal supervision via contrastive objectives. Both methods address this through pseudo-pair mining with bidirectional retrieval and weighted InfoNCE, but they differ in directness. Method A layers many components (copula modeling, listwise ranking, Gaussian-mixture marginals, asymmetric MAE pretraining) that address peripheral aspects of representation quality rather than the core failure mode of exploiting unimodal data for cross-modal contrastive learning. Method B more directly and coherently traces from the problem (scarce pairs, abundant unimodal data) to specific mechanisms: EMA teacher-based mutual top-k retrieval for pseudo-pair generation, uncertainty-weighted InfoNCE, and the mix-and-separate auxiliary that explicitly trains cross-modal association from independently drawn unimodal samples—all tightly coupled to the stated failure mode. Method B's causal pathway from problem to method steps is cleaner and more traceable.

Method A's strongest novel element is the copula-based compatibility scoring: fitting a parametric copula on real paired embeddings to weight pseudo-positives in the contrastive loss is an unusual and non-trivial mechanism for cross-modal learning. Method B also uses a copula (Gaussian-mixture marginals + parametric copula + variational ELBO), but additionally introduces a listwise ranking objective over mined candidate lists and a text-only LLM judge for scoring pseudo-pairs without gold supervision. Both methods share the copula idea, but Method B's combination of listwise contrastive learning over mined rankings (converting ranked candidate lists into a differentiable listwise objective) with the LLM-as-judge scoring mechanism for pseudo-pair quality is a more distinctive and harder-to-find combination in the cross-modal representation learning literature. Method A's novelty is largely the copula weighting, while Method B layers the copula with listwise ranking signals and an LLM judge, making its strongest novel element slightly more original in aggregate.

The failure mode is the scarcity of aligned text-image pairs combined with abundant unimodal data, requiring a mechanism that directly converts unimodal data into cross-modal supervision via contrastive objectives. Method A addresses this by synthesizing missing modalities (captioning images, retrieving/generating images for text) and weighting pseudo-pairs with a copula fitted on real pairs, creating a traceable pathway from unimodal data to cross-modal contrastive training. Method B addresses it through pseudo-pair mining via retrieval, unimodal contrastive shaping of marginals, and a copula-based distributional coupling that explicitly uses unpaired singletons in a joint ELBO. Both methods engage the failure mode, but Method B more directly and comprehensively addresses the core challenge: it explicitly couples unmatched unimodal singletons into the cross-modal objective through the copula ELBO with marginal likelihood terms on unpaired data, and its iterative mining-training loop with a lightweight judge provides a cleaner causal pathway from abundant unimodal pools to cross-modal contrastive supervision without requiring synthesis of the missing modality.

Method A's strongest novel element is the asymmetric two-stream MAE with 100% token utilization across diverse contexts per token, combined with a compute-aware data mixture using pool-specific utility exponents and repetition half-lives — the latter being a fairly distinctive scheduling mechanism. Method B's strongest novel element is the latentization-first strategy combining vector-quantized codebooks with a continuous residual pathway, plus heteroscedastic uncertainty modeling and an optional diffusion-style denoising head integrated into the masked pretraining objective. Both methods share many common elements (structure-aware masking, multi-target objectives, curriculum scheduling, contrastive terms). Method B's VQ codebook with residual pathway for avoiding trivial reconstruction is a more clearly differentiated and non-obvious design choice compared to prior masked pretraining work, and the combination of discrete latent targets + heteroscedastic continuous residuals + diffusion denoising head represents a more distinctive objective design. Method A's compute-aware data mixture scheduling is interesting but feels more like an engineering contribution. On balance, Method B's latentization-first tokenization strategy with the dual discrete/continuous pathway is the harder-to-find combination in prior literature.

The failure mode identified in the query is the challenge of adapting masked-prediction pretraining to non-language/vision data types (graphs, time series, sensor streams), specifically asking what objective design choices matter. Both methods directly engage this failure mode by proposing domain-aware tokenization, structure-aware masking strategies, and multi-target reconstruction objectives for each data type. Method A addresses it through curriculum-scheduled masking, asymmetric MAE design, and multi-target reconstruction with anti-collapse terms, with traceable pathways from each domain's inductive biases to specific masking and decoding choices. Method B addresses it through latentization-first tokenization (VQ codebook), structure-aware multiscale masking, heteroscedastic losses, and modality adapters, also with traceable pathways. Both methods engage the failure mode with comparable directness—each provides domain-specific masking strategies, objective design choices (reconstruction targets, contrastive terms, uncertainty modeling), and explicit discussion of what design choices matter. Neither method is clearly more direct than the other in resolving the named failure mode.

Method A's strongest novel element is the Neural CDE-based continuous-time stream encoder that can be queried at arbitrary timestamps for irregular/asynchronous data, combined with cross-structural corruption that simultaneously masks a node's time series and its incident edges, forcing joint temporal-relational recovery. The prototype-based confidence head for calibrated imputation is also a relatively uncommon component. Method B's strongest novel element is the asymmetric MAE with a two-stream, position-specific attention mask guaranteeing 100% token utilization across diverse corruption views, combined with data-curation utility exponents and repetition half-lives for compute-aware mixture planning. Both methods combine several existing ideas (span masking, contrastive objectives, curriculum scheduling), but Method A's use of Neural CDEs as a backbone component for irregular sensor streams in a masked-prediction framework is a more distinctive and harder-to-find combination than Method B's asymmetric MAE extension (which closely follows existing MAE/data2vec patterns applied to new domains). The cross-structural corruption coupling graph and temporal masking is also a more original mechanism than STAMP's largely incremental extensions of known techniques.

The failure mode identified in the query is the challenge of adapting masked-prediction pretraining—which works well for text and images—to other data types (graphs, time series, sensor streams), with specific attention to objective design choices that matter. Both methods directly engage this failure mode by proposing domain-aware masking strategies and tailored reconstruction objectives for graphs, time series, and sensor streams. Method A addresses it through specialized encoders (Graph Transformer and Neural CDE), domain-matched probabilistic losses, and cross-structural corruption that forces joint temporal-relational reasoning. Method B addresses it through a unified transformer backbone with domain-aware tokenization, curriculum-scheduled masking aligned to domain inductive biases, and multi-target reconstruction with spectral and derivative targets. Both trace a clear causal pathway from the problem (domain mismatch in masking and objectives) to specific method steps. However, Method B more directly addresses the "objective design choices that matter" aspect of the query by explicitly detailing how masking ratios, span distributions, asymmetric MAE design, spectral targets, and anti-collapse terms are chosen to match domain properties, while also addressing data mixture and compute-aware training. Method A is comparably thorough but spends more design effort on architectural novelty (Neural CDE, prototype confidence heads) rather than the objective design choices the query specifically highlights.

Method A's strongest novel element is the energy-based cloze loss with conditional NCE combined with a jointly-trained in-distribution proposer network (q) that generates context-aware replacements rather than random noise, plus the prefix-marginal contrast term to mitigate myopic gradients in long sequences. The proposer-model co-training loop with temperature control and the prefix-marginal auxiliary are non-standard combinations not commonly seen in masked pretraining literature. Method B's strongest novel element is the asymmetric two-stream MAE with 100% token utilization and the compute-aware data mixture planning with pool-specific utility exponents and repetition half-lives. However, the asymmetric MAE is closely related to existing MAE/BEiT variants, and the data mixture scheduling, while practical, is an engineering contribution rather than a mechanistic novelty. Method A's energy-based NCE objective with an in-distribution proposer network and the prefix-marginal contrast term represent a more original combination of mechanisms that would be harder to find in prior masked pretraining literature, whereas Method B's contributions are more incremental extensions of established MAE and contrastive learning ideas.

The failure mode identified in the query is the challenge of adapting masked-prediction pretraining to non-standard data types (graphs, time series, sensor streams) with appropriate objective design choices. Both methods address this directly by proposing domain-aware masking strategies and tailored objectives. Method A traces a clear causal pathway from each domain's inductive biases to specific masking choices (BFS subgraph masking for graphs, blockwise span masking for time series, channel-group masking for sensors) and to reconstruction objectives (node/edge attributes, derivatives at multiple horizons, spectral targets). Method B similarly addresses domain-specific masking but centers its core mechanism on an energy-based contrastive loss with a proposer network generating in-distribution replacements—this is a valid approach but introduces an additional mechanism (the proposer/NCE framework) that partially shifts focus away from the masked-prediction principle itself toward a discriminative energy-based paradigm. Method A more directly engages the query's framing of "masked-prediction" as the central principle and traces objective design choices more explicitly to each domain's structure, making its problem-method fit slightly more direct.

Method A's strongest novel element is the contextual propensity reweighting mechanism that treats each generator/augmentation policy as a distinct ranker and uses implicit interventions from multiple generator policies to fit examination models for importance weighting — this is a non-trivial borrowing from causal inference/debiasing literature applied to synthetic pretraining data alignment. Method B's strongest novel element is the doubly-robust estimator combining short-horizon fine-tune deltas with gradient similarity/influence metrics to predict downstream shard utility without full retrains, combined with constrained Bayesian optimization over mixture weights with hard no-regression safety constraints. Both methods are elaborate combinations of existing techniques, but Method B's doubly-robust utility estimation for online curriculum optimization (combining influence functions with LoRA-based pilot evaluations under formal no-regression constraints) is a more distinctly named and mechanistically specific contribution that would be harder to find in prior work as a unified component. Method A's propensity reweighting is conceptually interesting but draws more directly from established off-policy/importance-weighting literature without a clearly novel combination.

The failure mode is twofold: (a) synthetic data may not effectively exercise specific capabilities, and (b) synthetic data may degrade downstream performance relative to real data. Method A addresses both through a constrained Bayesian/bandit optimization loop with explicit no-regression constraints on held-out real data, influence-based utility estimation, and hard safety constraints enforcing epsilon-bounded regression—directly tracing from the degradation risk to specific mechanism steps. Method B addresses both through multi-judge quality filtering, contextual propensity reweighting to align synthetic distribution to real evaluation contexts, a real-data anchor, and online bandit gating—also directly tracing the causal pathway. Both methods engage the failure mode with comparable directness: Method A's strength is the formal constrained optimization with regression guards; Method B's strength is the propensity reweighting mechanism that directly addresses distribution mismatch between synthetic and real data. The mechanisms are similarly traceable and neither is clearly more direct than the other in resolving the core failure modes.

Method A's strongest novel element is the pairwise A/B Bradley–Terry preference evaluation framework applied at the batch level during pretraining, combined with domain-aware adapter architecture that separates synthetic artifacts from transferable skills during scaffold-to-real annealing. Method B's strongest novel element is contextual propensity reweighting treating each generator as a distinct ranker and fitting an examination model to estimate real-data likelihood, combined with consistency regularization across paraphrased views and precomputed teacher logit replay. Both methods share many common elements (capability graphs, quality filtering, curriculum scheduling, online bandit weighting). Method A's Bradley–Terry batch-level preference gating is a somewhat novel framing borrowed from RLHF applied to pretraining data selection, while Method B's propensity reweighting using implicit interventions from multiple generator policies to align synthetic to real distributions is a more technically distinctive mechanism not commonly seen in this context. However, both methods are largely assemblages of existing ideas. The propensity/examination model reweighting in Method B is a more specific and less obvious mechanism than the A/B preference testing in Method A, giving Method B a slight edge in novelty.

The failure mode is twofold: (1) replacing real pretraining data with synthetic data risks degrading downstream performance, and (2) synthetic data must be verified to actually exercise the intended capabilities. Method A addresses this through multi-judge quality filtering, contextual propensity reweighting to align synthetic distribution to real evaluation contexts, a real-data anchor, and an online bandit that monitors sentinel dev sets to gate synthetic streams—each step has a traceable causal pathway from the degradation risk to a specific mechanism. Method B addresses it through pairwise A/B evaluations with Bradley–Terry scoring to gate curriculum changes, domain-aware adapters to isolate synthetic artifacts, and uncertainty-weighted mixture updates. Both methods engage the failure mode directly, but Method B's pairwise A/B gating mechanism is particularly direct: it explicitly tests whether a synthetic batch improves or degrades downstream performance before admitting it, which is the most direct causal pathway to the "improves rather than degrades" concern. Method A's propensity reweighting and bandit are also direct but more indirect in that they reweight rather than gate. However, Method A's combination of mechanisms (quality gate, distribution alignment via propensity reweighting, real-data anchor, and online monitoring) collectively address both the capability-targeting and degradation-prevention aspects with comparable or slightly greater breadth and traceability. The two methods are comparably direct overall, with Method B slightly more direct on the degradation-prevention side and Method A slightly more comprehensive on the capability-targeting side.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of contrastive perturbation pairs with bias probes (flipping surface tokens while preserving semantics) plus online on-policy refinement where verifier partial-credit signals drive iterative item improvement, and bandit-style scheduling with rollback/acceptance gates. These are individually known ideas assembled into a coherent pipeline, but the specific combination of shortcut-detection probes with contrastive curriculum construction is somewhat distinctive. Method B's strongest novel element is contextual propensity reweighting: treating each generator/augmentation policy as a distinct ranker and fitting an examination model to estimate how likely an item would have been observed under real data collection, then using implicit interventions from multiple generator policies to compute importance weights. This causal/propensity-score framing applied to synthetic data distribution alignment in pretraining is a more original mechanism not commonly seen in this exact form. Both methods share many overlapping components (bandit scheduling, quality filtering, teacher distillation, real-data anchoring), but Method B's propensity reweighting mechanism and the consistency regularizer over paraphrase representations represent a more distinctive conceptual contribution compared to Method A's more incremental assembly of known techniques.

The failure mode is that synthetic pretraining data may degrade downstream performance by introducing distribution shift, artifacts, or capability gaps relative to real data. Both methods address this directly. Method A engages it through multi-judge quality filtering, contextual propensity reweighting (aligning synthetic distribution to real evaluation contexts), a real-data anchor, and an online bandit that gates synthetic streams based on sentinel dev set performance. Method B engages it through bias probes with contrastive counterfactuals, semantic-consistency filtering, programmatic verifiers, continual-learning regularizers with rehearsal, and a strict acceptance gate that only admits synthetic batches if they yield non-decreasing performance on real-data dev sets. Both trace a clear causal pathway from the problem (synthetic data degrading performance) to specific method steps. However, Method B's acceptance gate in Stage 4—which explicitly requires non-decreasing real-data dev performance before admitting synthetic batches to the curriculum—is a more direct and conservative mechanism for ensuring synthetic data improves rather than degrades performance. Method B also adds contrastive bias probes that specifically detect shortcut learning, which is a concrete failure mode of synthetic data. Method A's propensity reweighting is sophisticated but more indirect. Overall the two methods are comparably direct, with Method B having a slightly more explicit acceptance/rejection mechanism tied to real-data performance.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of sparse autoencoder decomposition with submodular greedy search for minimal sufficient sets, Shapley-style multi-component attributions, and fitting a structural causal model (SCM) over discovered components validated by interventional prediction. This is a fairly comprehensive but largely assembled pipeline from existing techniques (activation patching, SAEs, Shapley values, SCMs). Method B's strongest novel element is the "calibrated intervention scale" concept—standardizing all necessity/sufficiency tests to a fixed KL divergence budget so components are compared at equivalent causal strength—combined with the "causal competition" mechanism where components compete over a shared intervention budget to resolve overlapping candidates, and the uncertainty-driven probe planner that maximizes disagreement among causal attributions. The KL-calibrated intervention standardization and the competition-based disambiguation are more distinctively original framings not commonly found in mechanistic interpretability literature, whereas Method A's components (activation patching, SAEs, Shapley, SCMs) are individually well-established and their combination, while thorough, is more predictable.

The failure mode is that correlation between internal components and capabilities does not establish causation, so the challenge is to identify which components *causally* implement a capability and leverage that for editing/interpretation. Both methods directly engage this failure mode through interventional (patching/knockout/knock-in) approaches that go beyond correlation. Method A traces a clear causal pathway: counterfactual datasets → targeted interchange interventions measuring causal effects → minimal sufficient set identification → structural causal model fitting → editing/interpretation using the validated causal map. Method B similarly uses necessity/sufficiency tests, calibrated KL-budget interventions, and competition tests to resolve overlapping candidates. Both hit the failure mode head-on. However, Method A's explicit step of fitting a structural causal model validated by predicting outcomes of *unseen* interventions provides a more rigorous causal (not merely interventional) framework, and its greedy search for minimal sufficient sets with Shapley-style attributions more directly addresses the "correlation vs. causation" distinction. Method B's KL-budget calibration is a useful refinement but is more of an engineering detail than a deeper engagement with the causal identification problem. The causal pathway in Method A is more completely traced from problem to solution.

Method A's strongest novel element is the calibration-aware intervention framework that standardizes all interventions to a fixed KL-divergence budget, enabling fair cross-component comparisons, combined with uncertainty-driven probe generation and causal competition tests with top-down occlusion ordering. Method B's strongest novel element is the information-bottleneck gating combined with sparse circuit screening as a preprocessing step before causal validation, plus a Bayesian posterior over SCM edges with active intervention selection. Both methods combine established techniques (activation patching, necessity/sufficiency testing, sparse gating, SCM framing) in non-trivial ways. Method A's KL-calibrated intervention scale and causal competition/priority ordering is a somewhat distinctive framing, but Method B's combination of information-bottleneck-style rate penalties on gated activations for circuit discovery, followed by Bayesian active intervention selection over an explicit SCM, represents a more original combination of mechanisms not commonly seen together in this literature. The IB-gating for circuit screening is a particularly non-obvious addition to the causal interpretability pipeline.

The failure mode is that correlation between internal components and capabilities does not establish causation, so we need methods that directly identify causal components (not just correlated ones) and leverage that for editing/interpretation. Both methods engage this failure mode directly: Method A uses calibrated necessity/sufficiency interventions with standardized KL budgets, competition tests to disambiguate overlapping candidates, and gated editing with preservation constraints. Method B uses sparse circuit screening followed by explicit structural causal model (SCM) interventions with activation patching, Shapley decomposition for interactions, and Bayesian active intervention selection. Both trace a clear causal pathway from the problem (correlation ≠ causation) to specific interventional steps. However, Method B's explicit SCM framing, randomized controlled activation patching described as "do-interventions," Bayesian posterior over causal effects with active stopping criteria, and the separation of screening from causal validation create a slightly more rigorous and traceable causal identification pipeline. Method A's calibration-aware approach is also strong but its causal validation is somewhat less formally grounded in causal inference machinery. The difference is marginal, making this essentially a tie in directness of engagement.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of information-gain-based layer localization (using conditional information gain to identify the earliest layer where capability emerges) with KL-calibrated knock-in/knock-out interventions and mediated effect patching on a shortcut-controlled testbed. Method B's strongest novel element is the uncertainty-driven, disagreement-maximizing probe generation loop (an active learning planner that proposes new probes based on disagreement among current causal attributions) combined with causal competition tests that allocate a fixed intervention budget for pairwise/group-wise component competitions with top-down occlusion/priority ordering. The active-learning probe planner and the formalized causal competition/priority-ordering mechanism in Method B are more distinctly novel combinations not commonly found together in mechanistic interpretability literature, whereas Method A's components (SAE features, activation patching, KL-calibrated interventions, greedy circuit selection) are closer to existing published work in circuit-finding and causal tracing. Method B's causal competition framework with iterative top-down correction is the harder element to surface in prior literature.

The failure mode is that correlation between internal components and capabilities does not establish causation, making it unclear which components actually implement a capability and how to edit or interpret them. Both methods directly engage this failure mode through calibrated interventional testing (necessity/sufficiency knockouts and patch-ins), controlled contrastive probe sets to avoid spurious correlations, and component-level causal attribution followed by targeted editing. Method A adds a layer-localization step (information gain per layer) before drilling into components, which is a reasonable preprocessing step but somewhat peripheral to the core causal identification problem. Method B more directly and systematically addresses the failure mode by explicitly framing necessity and sufficiency tests as the primary mechanism, adding causal competition tests to disambiguate overlapping candidates (a specific sub-problem when multiple components co-vary), and iterating probe construction to avoid spurious correlations—all of which trace more directly to the correlation-vs-causation gap. Both methods are highly comparable in directness, but Method B's explicit necessity/sufficiency framing and causal competition step more precisely target the correlation-causation distinction.

Method A's strongest novel element is the batch-wise counterfactual augmentation with feature-swapping (swapping feature vectors across examples while keeping environments fixed) combined with a separator network that softly assigns activations to features or an "environment" pool, plus multi-view readouts. These are interesting but draw heavily from existing causal representation learning (e.g., IRM, causal disentanglement) and sparse dictionary methods. Method B's strongest novel element is the "Executable Causal Dictionaries" framing: the combination of a bilevel circuit selection problem with bootstrap stability, a feature-level surrogate that must reconstruct activations AND preserve outputs when substituted back into the live model, automatic generation of executable predicate-based test suites with hard negatives via differentiable perturbations, and adversarial falsification that iteratively refines circuit boundaries. The substitution fidelity check (replacing the circuit with its surrogate in the running model) combined with automatically induced executable hypotheses from a predicate library is a more distinctive and harder-to-find combination than Method A's mechanisms, which are closer to established causal disentanglement and probing literature.

The failure mode is that learned representations are opaque, making it hard to (a) reverse-engineer what circuits compute, (b) decompose representations into human-interpretable features, and (c) verify those decompositions. Both methods address all three stages. Method A uses gradient-weighted relevance maps for circuit localization, counterfactual-augmented sparse factorization for decomposition, and ablation/patching/surrogate tests for verification. Method B uses interventional influence mapping with a bilevel minimization for circuit isolation, a sparse overcomplete dictionary with disentangling pressures for decomposition, and substitution fidelity, interventional parity, and adversarial falsification for verification. Method B's circuit isolation step is more directly mechanistic—it uses activation replacement along candidate paths and a formal bilevel selection problem to find the minimal causal subgraph, which more directly addresses the "reverse-engineer what circuits compute" part of the failure mode. Method B's verification via substitution fidelity (replacing the circuit with its surrogate in the live model) and adversarial falsification provides a more traceable causal pathway from problem to verification step than Method A's aggregated multi-signal approach. Both are strong, but Method B's mechanisms more directly and precisely engage each named sub-challenge.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of a Bayesian structural prior over circuit graphs with a distinction-maximization prototype head trained via a mix-and-separate objective, plus a Bradley-Terry pairwise verification scheme with a "difference-evidence" metric. The mix-and-separate disentanglement objective applied to circuit decomposition is a relatively distinctive framing. Method B's strongest novel element is the separator network with batch-wise counterfactual feature-swapping (swapping feature vectors across examples while keeping environments fixed) combined with multi-view readout tokens and a surrogate fidelity check via gradient-boosted trees with SHAP attributions. The counterfactual invariance training (swap features/environments) in Method B echoes IRM/causal representation learning literature fairly directly, and the surrogate+SHAP verification is well-established. Method A's Bayesian structural prior over architectural-constrained circuit graphs combined with the prototype mix-and-separate objective and Bradley-Terry causal verification represents a less standard combination than Method B's more recognizable assembly of counterfactual augmentation, probing, ablation/patching, and SHAP—all of which are individually well-known mechanistic interpretability tools.

The failure mode is that learned representations are opaque, making it hard to (a) reverse-engineer what circuits compute, (b) decompose representations into human-interpretable features, and (c) verify those decompositions. Both methods address all three sub-problems with explicit mechanisms: circuit discovery via gradient/ablation screening, feature decomposition via sparse factorization or prototype heads, and verification via necessity/sufficiency interventions. Method A traces a causal pathway from the opacity problem through counterfactual augmentations that enforce causal sufficiency of features, ablation-based necessity/sufficiency tests, and a surrogate fidelity check—each step directly targeting the named failure modes. Method B similarly covers circuit discovery (Bayesian structural prior + ablation screening), decomposition (prototype head with mix-and-separate objective), and verification (preregistered randomized intervention tests with Bradley-Terry ranking). Both methods engage the failure mode with comparable directness and traceable causal pathways from problem to method steps; neither is clearly more direct than the other in addressing the core triad of reverse-engineering, decomposition, and verification.

Method A's strongest novel element is the goal-directed backward-chaining circuit extraction with path attribution and the in-distribution feature marginalization via a conditional co-activation model p(z_i | z_-i) built from SAE codes — this combination of backward-chaining over computation graphs with SAE-based interventions is a fairly distinctive framing. Method B's strongest novel element is the separator network with batch-wise counterfactual augmentations that enforce causal sufficiency/necessity during training (swapping feature vectors across examples while keeping environments fixed), combined with a competition step for contested units and a surrogate fidelity check via gradient-boosted trees with SHAP. Both methods combine existing ideas (SAEs, activation patching, counterfactual interventions) in moderately novel ways. Method B's separator network with counterfactual invariance training as a learning objective — enforcing that swapped features must preserve predictions if and only if they are causal — is a more distinctive architectural/training mechanism not commonly seen in mechanistic interpretability pipelines. Method A's backward-chaining search is creative but closer to existing attribution/circuit-finding literature. Method B's causal invariance training signal during decomposition is the harder-to-find combination.

The failure mode is that learned representations are opaque, making it hard to reverse-engineer circuits, decompose representations into interpretable features, and verify those decompositions. Method A directly addresses all three sub-problems with a traceable causal pathway: sparse autoencoders for decomposition, backward-chaining path attribution for circuit extraction, and interventional patching with in-distribution counterfactuals for verification. Each step mechanistically targets the opacity problem—SAEs decompose activations into interpretable features, goal-directed search identifies minimal circuits, and causal interventions verify edge weights. Method B also addresses the failure mode through a separator network for feature factorization, counterfactual augmentations for causal sufficiency, and ablation/patching for verification. However, Method B's mechanism for circuit reverse-engineering is less direct—it relies on gradient-weighted attention maps for coarse localization rather than a systematic backward-chaining circuit extraction, and its verification aggregates multiple signals via a surrogate model rather than directly testing causal pathways. Method A's backward-chaining expansion with path attribution more directly engages the "reverse-engineer what specific circuits compute" aspect of the failure mode, and its in-distribution intervention strategy more directly addresses the verification challenge.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of active probing to build a synthetic anchor bank, orthogonal projection of update directions relative to sensitivity directions computed on that bank, and importance-weighted consolidation that folds adapters only along low-importance directions. The orthogonal projection constrained by a dynamically generated anchor bank is a distinctive mechanism. Method B's strongest novel element is the conflict-aware gating that down-weights the KL preservation loss by teacher uncertainty when gradients conflict, combined with purely additive residual pathways and a data-free anchor stream. However, the core ideas in Method B—frozen backbone with residual adapters, teacher-student KL distillation on a small coreset, and conflict-aware gradient weighting—are individually well-established and their combination is relatively straightforward. Method A's orthogonal gradient projection tied to an actively probed, diversity-maximized anchor bank is a more distinctive and harder-to-find combination, even if components like EWC-style importance and distillation exist separately.

The failure mode is catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning on new tasks, without access to prior data. Both methods engage this directly: Method A uses frozen backbone + trainable residual adapters + teacher-student KL on synthetic anchors to preserve old behaviors while learning new ones. Method B uses low-rank adapters + distillation on a probed anchor bank + orthogonal gradient projection to prevent interference with prior skill directions. Both hit the failure mode head-on with traceable causal pathways. However, Method B adds an explicit orthogonal projection mechanism that directly prevents gradient updates from interfering with directions critical to old skills—this is a more direct mechanical engagement with the forgetting failure mode (updates are geometrically constrained away from prior-skill directions). Method A's primary protection is architectural (frozen weights), which is also direct, but the residual path still receives gradients that could theoretically drift. Both are comparably direct, but Method B's orthogonality constraint provides an additional, more targeted anti-forgetting mechanism beyond just freezing weights.

Method A's strongest novel element is the confidence-gated plasticity mechanism that dynamically scales adapter learning rates and distillation strength based on a deterministic uncertainty head, combined with gradient-reversal domain alignment between student and teacher features. However, each individual component (EWC, LoRA/adapters, knowledge distillation, prototypes, contrastive learning, gradient reversal, confidence gating) is a well-established technique, and the combination, while elaborate, reads as an engineering assembly of known parts rather than a genuinely new mechanism. Method B's strongest novel element is the "conflict-aware preservation" gating where KL divergence is down-weighted specifically when new-task gradients conflict with anchor gradients, combined with the data-free anchor stream generated by the teacher itself as a substitute for stored data. This gradient-conflict detection as a signal to modulate retention pressure is a somewhat more targeted and less commonly combined mechanism, though it too draws on known components. Both methods are largely assemblies of existing techniques; Method A is more verbose and combines more components, but Method B's conflict-aware gradient gating tied to teacher-generated anchors as the primary replay mechanism represents a slightly more focused and less precedented combination.

The failure mode is catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning on new tasks without access to prior data. Both methods engage this failure mode directly. Method A uses a combination of frozen teacher distillation, prototype-based pseudo-replay, confidence-gated plasticity, EWC-style importance penalties, and adversarial alignment—all aimed at preserving prior capabilities without storing old data. Method B uses a frozen backbone with additive residual adapters, teacher-student KL distillation on anchor streams (data-free or tiny coreset), and conflict-aware gradient gating. Both trace a clear causal pathway from the problem (forgetting during new-task updates) to specific mechanisms (frozen paths, distillation, no full prior data retention). However, Method B's core mechanism—keeping the backbone entirely frozen and making new learning purely additive through residual modules—more directly and structurally prevents forgetting by architectural design: the old capabilities literally cannot be overwritten because those weights are never updated. Method A's backbone is also largely frozen, but it introduces more peripheral components (adversarial alignment, domain discriminators, uncertainty gating) that address secondary concerns rather than the core forgetting mechanism. Method B's mechanism is more parsimonious and directly targets the failure mode with a traceable causal pathway.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of self-synthesized rehearsal (using the frozen base model seeded with task descriptors to generate synthetic proxy data) with per-layer gating scalars α(x,l) that adaptively suppress or activate new adapters based on input-skill similarity, plus a periodic consolidation step via multi-skill self-synthesized distillation. Method B's strongest novel element is the conflict-aware preservation mechanism that down-weights KL divergence by teacher uncertainty when gradients conflict, combined with a data-free anchor stream and k-center coreset selection. Both methods share a large common core: frozen backbone, residual/adapter modules, teacher-student KL on anchors, and periodic consolidation. Method A's self-synthesized rehearsal pipeline (generating synthetic proxy data from task descriptors without any stored examples) and the input-routing gate that dynamically modulates adapter influence per layer represent a more distinctive and harder-to-find combination than Method B's conflict-aware KL weighting, which is a relatively incremental extension of standard EWC/distillation ideas. Method A's synthesis-based memory construction is the more original mechanism.

The failure mode is catastrophic forgetting when fine-tuning on new tasks without access to prior data. Both methods engage this failure mode through similar mechanisms: freezing the backbone, using adapter/residual modules for new tasks, and generating synthetic or anchor-based rehearsal data to approximate prior distributions without storing original data. Method A uses a frozen teacher with a KL divergence objective on anchor batches (data-free anchors + tiny coreset) to preserve prior behaviors, with conflict-aware gradient weighting. Method B uses self-synthesized rehearsal generated by the frozen base model, per-input gating to suppress new adapters when prior skills suffice, and a similar KL objective. Both trace a clear causal pathway from the forgetting problem to specific method steps. The engagement is comparably direct: both freeze the backbone to preserve prior weights, both use lightweight adapters to localize new learning, and both approximate prior data distributions synthetically to enforce retention. Neither method has a meaningfully more direct engagement with the core failure mode than the other.

Method A's strongest novel element is the Pareto-front selection over quality, uncertainty, and novelty axes combined with density-ratio importance weighting and a rollback/gating mechanism — but each of these components (submodular diversity selection, KL-proximal constraints, replay buffers, ensemble uncertainty) is individually well-established in self-training, active learning, and RLHF literature. The combination is systematic but not surprising. Method B's strongest novel element is the "regulated diversity" projection f(·) that explicitly partitions which dimensions should diversify versus remain stable, combined with rank-based filtration that deliberately samples from reliable extremes (top and occasional bottom) of a candidate slate, plus interventional propensity reweighting using logs from multiple checkpoints to debias filter-gated data. The rank-weight vector emphasizing extremes and de-emphasizing the noisy middle is a less standard mechanism, and the interventional propensity correction framing (borrowing from causal inference) applied specifically to self-training filter bias is a more distinctive combination than what Method A offers. Both methods are largely assemblages of known techniques, but Method B's interventional propensity reweighting and the regulated-diversity projection concept represent a slightly more original framing not easily found as a unified prior work.

The failure mode is distributional collapse during self-training: reinforcing existing errors and narrowing coverage when filtering model-generated data for reuse. Method A addresses this through calibrated evaluators, inverse propensity weighting, diversity regulation via a projection function, novelty scoring, and collapse detection with automatic corrective responses (raising exploration floor, widening diversity). Method B addresses it through Pareto-front selection over quality/uncertainty/novelty, submodular diversity-aware subset selection, KL proximal constraints, counterfactual pairs, density-ratio importance weighting, per-bucket quotas, and an explicit rollback/gating mechanism tied to distributional indicators. Both methods engage the failure mode directly and with traceable causal pathways. However, Method B's rollback/gating mechanism—which explicitly prevents a collapsed model from becoming the next teacher—is a more direct structural safeguard against the reinforcement-of-errors aspect of the failure mode, and its combination of KL constraint, counterfactual pairs, and canary monitoring provides comparably direct coverage of distributional collapse. The two methods are essentially equivalent in directness of engagement with the failure mode, each hitting both sub-problems (error reinforcement and coverage collapse) through multiple complementary mechanisms of similar specificity.

Method A's strongest novel element is the inverse propensity scoring mechanism applied to filter-gated self-generated data—treating the selection pipeline as an intervention and reweighting losses by estimated propensity scores from interventional logs across multiple checkpoints. This causal-inference framing applied to self-training data selection is a non-trivial combination not commonly seen in this literature. Method B's strongest novel element is the retrieval-anchored candidate evaluation combined with pairwise tournaments and a canary-set-based scheduler that pauses pseudo-label ingestion. However, retrieval-augmented verification, EMA teacher-student setups, DAgger-style oracle queries, and pairwise ranking losses are all well-established components whose combination, while competent, is more easily surfaced in prior work on semi-supervised and self-training literature. Method A's propensity-reweighting of on-policy filtered data to produce approximately unbiased supervision is a more original mechanism that crosses causal inference methodology into self-training in a way that is harder to find as a prior published combination.

The failure mode is distributional collapse when bootstrapping from self-generated data filtered by a quality signal — specifically, the risk that the model reinforces its own errors and narrows its output distribution. Method A engages this through EMA teacher-student separation, retrieval-anchored checks, pairwise ranking loss, coverage constraints with distinctiveness filters, counterfactual synthesis, DAgger-style oracle intervention, KL/entropy regularization against a frozen base model, and canary-set monitoring with entropy floor checks — each step traceable to either the "reinforcing errors" or "distributional collapse" failure mode. Method B engages it through calibrated graded evaluators, inverse propensity reweighting to correct selection-feedback bias, novelty scoring in a coverage space, dynamic bucket scheduling with collapse indicators, and a diversity regulator — also traceable but with some steps (e.g., targeted refinement of ambiguous spans, auxiliary heads) addressing peripheral efficiency concerns rather than the core failure mode. Both methods directly address the failure mode, but Method A's mechanisms more comprehensively and explicitly target both sub-problems (error reinforcement via retrieval anchoring and oracle correction; distributional collapse via entropy regularization, coverage constraints, and canary monitoring), while Method B's inverse propensity reweighting is a notably direct mechanism for the selection-feedback bias aspect that Method A handles less explicitly. Overall the directness of engagement is comparable, with Method A slightly more thorough on collapse prevention and Method B slightly more principled on bias correction.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of multi-snapshot candidate pools, agreement-under-perturbation consistency checks across semantic view sets, and influence-function-based post-hoc filtering against a golden set — but each of these components (self-training with frozen snapshots, consistency regularization, influence functions) exists in prior literature and their combination, while thoughtful, is not a single clearly unprecedented mechanism. Method B's strongest novel element is the inverse propensity reweighting applied specifically to filter-gated self-generated data — treating the self-training selection process as an intervention and correcting for selection bias using propensity scores estimated from multi-checkpoint interventional logs. This causal framing applied to the self-training loop (turning on-policy filtered data into approximately unbiased supervision) is a more distinctive and harder-to-find combination. The rank-weight vector emphasizing reliable extremes plus the novelty score ν integrated into importance sampling weights (μ^α · ν^β · g(σ)) also represents a more precisely specified and less standard mechanism than anything in Method A.

The failure mode is distributional collapse during self-training: reinforcing existing errors and narrowing coverage when filtering model-generated data. Method A directly addresses this through multiple traceable mechanisms: a regulated diversity projection f(·) that explicitly monitors which dimensions should diversify, inverse propensity weighting to correct selection-feedback bias, collapse indicators (coverage over f, rare-feature recall) with automatic corrective responses (raising εexplore), and a novelty score ν in the selection weighting. The causal pathway from "distributional collapse" to specific method steps is explicit and tight. Method B also addresses the failure mode through frozen snapshots, rehearsal buffers, OOD cluster mixing, and influence analysis, but these mechanisms are more diffuse and oriented toward general robustness rather than directly targeting the collapse pathway. Method A's collapse-detection-and-correction loop and its propensity-weighted correction of filter-induced bias more directly engage the named failure mode with clearer causal traceability.

Method A's strongest novel element is the offline perturbation bank concept—amortizing adversarial generation cost into a reusable, typed bank with hardness-aware sampling and curriculum scheduling during contrastive pretraining. However, this is a relatively straightforward combination of adversarial training, contrastive self-supervised learning (e.g., RoCL, ACL, AdvCL), and offline data augmentation caching—all well-established ideas whose combination has been explored in prior work on adversarially robust self-supervised learning.

Method B's strongest novel element is the ELECTRIC-style energy-based cloze objective combined with mixed-embedding interpolation (λ-scheduled blending of true and replaced token embeddings) and semantics-controlled corruption via SRL-guided seq2seq generation. The specific combination of an energy-based replaced-token detection objective with embedding-space interpolation annealing, semantic-control-code corruption, and multi-view [VIEW] token redundancy for NLP robustness is a more unusual assembly. The energy-based cloze + embedding mixing + semantic control codes combination is harder to find as a unified prior work, whereas Method A's combination maps more directly onto existing adversarially robust contrastive pretraining literature.

The failure mode is model brittleness to adversarial perturbations imperceptible to humans, and the query asks for pretraining objectives or data augmentation that build robustness without test-time defenses. Method A directly engages this failure mode: it explicitly generates imperceptible adversarial perturbations (gradient-based under perceptual constraints, L-inf/L2 bounds, LPIPS thresholds) and forces representation invariance to these worst-case perturbations via contrastive and consistency losses during pretraining, with evaluation against PGD/AutoAttack-style attacks. The causal pathway from adversarial brittleness → offline adversarial bank → invariance training → no test-time defense is clear and direct. Method B addresses a related but distinct problem: robustness to natural language surface-form variations (synonym swaps, tokenization jitters, casing changes) in NLP models. While it uses consistency losses and perturbation-based training, its perturbations are semantic/linguistic variations rather than adversarial imperceptible perturbations in the vision/gradient-attack sense the query targets. Method A's mechanism more directly resolves the named failure mode of adversarial imperceptibility and brittleness.

Method A's strongest novel element is the ELECTRIC-style energy-based cloze objective combined with semantics-preserving corruption generators and embedding interpolation scheduling—this is a meaningful combination but draws heavily from established components (ELECTRIC, consistency regularization, data augmentation with semantic controls) without a clearly unprecedented mechanism. Method B's strongest novel element is the combination of a variational information bottleneck with prototype-consistency regularization and gradient-reversal domain adversarial alignment, all operating on a split latent space (z_shared/z_pert) with uncertainty-weighted perturbation curricula. While both methods combine existing ideas, Method B's split-latent IB-prototype-gradient-reversal combination for adversarial robustness is a more unusual and harder-to-find configuration in the literature—the explicit disentanglement of perturbation-specific signals via gradient reversal paired with prototype consistency and IB compression is a more original architectural framing than Method A's augmented pretraining pipeline.

The failure mode is model brittleness to imperceptible adversarial perturbations, and the query asks for pretraining objectives or data augmentation that bake in robustness without test-time defenses. Method A addresses this through semantics-preserving corruptions at pretraining, an energy-based cloze objective that learns invariance to subtle token-level edits, and a consistency loss penalizing prediction drift under imperceptible perturbations—each step directly targets the brittleness to small, human-imperceptible changes. Method B also addresses the failure mode through perturbation-aware augmentation, an information bottleneck to remove adversarial shortcuts, and prototype consistency across clean and perturbed views, with a learned perturbation generator that explicitly challenges the representation. Both methods trace a clear causal pathway from the problem (brittleness to imperceptible perturbations) to specific pretraining mechanisms. However, Method B more directly engages the failure mode by explicitly generating adversarial (maximally challenging) perturbations via a learned prompt module, using gradient reversal to actively remove perturbation-specific signals from the shared representation, and imposing an information bottleneck to compress nuisance features that carry adversarial shortcuts—all of which more precisely target the adversarial brittleness mechanism rather than general semantic variation.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of a naturalness-constrained spatially varying exposure field with saliency-guided PGD perturbation generation inside a contrastive SSL framework, plus the "dataset reinforcement" mechanism that precomputes and stores teacher soft predictions over a perturbation bank for replay. However, each of these components (contrastive learning with adversarial augmentations, memory banks, knowledge distillation with KL alignment, PGD-guided hard negatives) is individually well-established, and their combination, while competent, follows a fairly predictable assembly of known SSL robustness techniques. Method B's strongest novel element is the ELECTRIC-style energy-based cloze objective applied specifically to semantics-preserving corruptions generated by a controlled seq2seq model with semantic control codes, combined with mixed-embedding annealing (λ-scheduled interpolation at replaced positions) to smooth brittleness around subtle edits. The energy-based discriminative pretraining applied to a carefully curated corruption distribution with semantic control codes and the embedding interpolation schedule represents a more distinctive and less precedented combination for this specific problem in NLP robustness pretraining.

The failure mode is model brittleness to adversarial/imperceptible perturbations, requiring robustness to be built into pretraining or data augmentation rather than test-time defenses. Method A addresses this through a text-domain pipeline combining semantics-preserving corruptions, energy-based cloze pretraining, consistency losses, and embedding smoothing—all traceable to the failure mode of sensitivity to imperceptible edits. Method B addresses the same failure mode in the image domain through perturbation-aware contrastive pretraining with naturalness-constrained augmentations, hard negative mining via lightweight PGD, and dataset reinforcement—each step directly targeting invariance to imperceptible perturbations baked into pretraining. Both methods directly engage the failure mode with clear causal pathways from the problem (brittleness to imperceptible perturbations) to specific pretraining steps (augmentation + invariance objectives), and both explicitly avoid test-time defenses. The engagement is comparably direct, with Method B's use of PGD-guided hard augmentations and dataset reinforcement being particularly targeted at the adversarial perturbation failure mode, while Method A's consistency and smoothing regularizer similarly targets prediction drift under imperceptible edits. The directness of engagement is essentially equivalent across both methods.

Method A's strongest novel element is the inverse propensity scoring (IPS) framework applied to preference logs, harvesting implicit interventions where the same prompt-response appeared at different ranks across historic policies to fit a contextual propensity model and debias selection artifacts. This is a relatively uncommon mechanism in reward modeling. Method B's strongest novel element is the hierarchical Bradley-Terry/Thurstone model with per-annotator confusion parameters and per-instance difficulty scalars trained via an EM-like procedure, combined with BALD-style active learning for pair selection. However, the hierarchical annotator reliability modeling (Dawid-Skene style) combined with active learning via BALD is a well-established combination in crowdsourcing and active learning literature. Method A's IPS-based debiasing of presentation propensity in preference logs is a more distinctive and less commonly applied mechanism in this specific reward modeling context, though both methods are largely assemblages of existing techniques. The propensity modeling from historical policy logs represents a more original framing for this problem than Method B's combination of established crowdsourcing and active learning components.

The failure mode is learning reliable reward models when human preference labels are scarce and/or noisy. Both methods engage this failure mode directly. Method A addresses it through: annotator reliability modeling (hierarchical Bradley-Terry with per-annotator confusion parameters), active learning via BALD-style information gain to maximize label efficiency, noise-robust loss functions, and semi-supervised augmentation with counterfactuals. Method B addresses it through: LLM judge ensembles with confidence-weighted labels, label triage based on confidence margins, IPS-style debiasing of selection artifacts, controlled perturbation synthesis for data densification, and heteroscedastic noise modeling in the ranking objective. Both methods trace a clear causal pathway from the problem (scarce/noisy labels) to specific mechanisms. However, Method A's causal pathway is more tightly integrated: the EM procedure directly models and corrects for annotator noise at the parameter level, the active learning component directly addresses label scarcity by maximizing information per label, and the uncertainty calibration directly addresses reliability. Method B's mechanisms are comparably comprehensive but the propensity/IPS component addresses a somewhat peripheral concern (selection bias in historical logs) rather than the core scarcity/noise problem. Both methods are roughly equivalent in directness of engagement with the core failure mode, making this very close to a tie, but Method A's annotator reliability modeling and active learning integration are slightly more directly mechanistically tied to the named failure modes of noise and scarcity.

Method A's strongest novel element is the variational latent decomposition that separates reward into a shared utility and annotator-specific bias components, combined with an uncertainty-guided EMA teacher-student scheme where the EMA update rate is modulated by per-example uncertainty. The round-based unification of pairwise and pointwise signals via Bradley-Terry token probabilities from a single generative judge is also a reasonably original framing. Method B's strongest novel element is the inverse propensity scoring (IPS) applied to preference logs by estimating context-dependent presentation propensity from historic policy logs—harvesting implicit interventions where the same prompt-response appeared at different ranks. However, IPS-style debiasing of preference data has appeared in prior work (e.g., in RLHF with logged bandit data), and the rest of Method B's components (ensemble judges, margin-based triage, controlled perturbations, heteroscedastic noise modeling) are well-established combinations. Method A's annotator-bias decomposition via a variational latent layer injected into reward modeling, combined with uncertainty-guided EMA rate adaptation, represents a more original mechanism not commonly found in this exact form in the reward modeling literature.

The failure mode is learning reliable reward models when high-quality human preference labels are scarce and noisy. Both methods engage this failure mode directly with multiple mechanisms. Method A addresses it through: multi-signal supervision combining scarce pairwise with abundant pointwise signals, pseudo-preference expansion via retrieval and simulation, uncertainty-calibrated teacher-student training with noise estimation, and a variational latent decomposition separating shared utility from annotator bias. Method B addresses it through: ensemble-based label aggregation with confidence weighting, triage of labels by confidence margin to route uncertain cases to humans, IPS-style debiasing of selection artifacts, controlled perturbation synthesis for data densification, and heteroscedastic noise modeling in the ranking objective. Both methods trace causal pathways from the problem (scarce/noisy labels) to specific mechanisms. However, Method A's latent decomposition of reward into shared utility and annotator-specific biases provides a more structurally direct mechanism for handling annotator noise, and its multi-signal unification more directly addresses the scarcity problem by leveraging weaker but abundant signals. Method B's propensity/IPS component addresses selection bias rather than the core scarcity/noise problem, making it somewhat less focused. Overall, both are comparably direct in engaging the failure mode, with Method A's annotator bias decomposition and Method B's triage/routing system each hitting the failure mode from slightly different but equally valid angles.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of margin-based elbow/knee detection with a multi-signal instance-weighting scheme (ensemble agreement × curriculum difficulty × judge fidelity probe × process-reward consistency) and a "fidelity probe" that tests an LLM judge's commitment to its chosen side — the fidelity probe concept is relatively uncommon in the literature. Method B's strongest novel element is the inverse propensity scoring (IPS) applied to historical policy logs to debias selection artifacts in preference data, combined with heteroscedastic noise modeling where per-pair likelihood temperature is set by propagated uncertainty. The IPS-based debiasing of implicit preference logs is a more distinctly novel mechanism for this specific problem — borrowing from causal inference/off-policy evaluation in a non-trivial way — whereas Method A's components (curriculum learning, ensemble weighting, LLM-as-judge confidence) are more individually established, even if combined in a new way. Method B's propensity modeling of presentation context and the heteroscedastic ranking objective together form a more original combination that is harder to find in existing reward model literature.

The failure mode is learning reliable reward models when human preference labels are scarce and noisy. Both methods engage this failure mode through similar mechanisms: confidence-weighted labeling, ensemble-based noise filtering, margin-based data triage, curriculum/difficulty-aware training, and routing uncertain examples to humans. Method A focuses on bootstrapping from existing data with noise-aware loss weighting, curriculum learning, and iterative refinement with a human budget. Method B adds an additional mechanism directly targeting a specific source of bias in preference data—selection/presentation bias in historical logs via inverse propensity scoring (IPS)—and also addresses verbosity/length confounders through explicit regularization and debiasing in the ranking objective. Both methods trace a clear causal pathway from the problem (scarce/noisy labels) to specific steps (filtering, weighting, human routing). However, Method B's explicit treatment of propensity/selection bias and confounder orthogonalization addresses additional failure modes that arise specifically in real-world preference datasets (not just label noise but systematic collection biases), making its engagement slightly more comprehensive and direct. The two methods are otherwise comparably direct in their core mechanisms, making this very close to a tie, but Method B's debiasing of selection artifacts and confounder regularization provide a marginally more complete engagement with the reliability problem.
